title: "Count Data Stochastic Frontier Models with Gamma Inefficiency"
thanks: "The opinions expressed are those of the authors and do not necessarily reflect the views of the IDB, its Board of Directors, or its member countries. It also stipulates that the IDB makes no warranties regarding the accuracy, reliability, or completeness of the information."
abstract: |
  We propose a stochastic frontier model for count data where the output follows a Poisson distribution conditional on a Gamma-distributed inefficiency term. The marginal probability mass function admits an absolutely convergent
  alternating-series representation in the production-frontier case and a one-dimensional integral evaluable by adaptive quadrature in the cost-frontier case, both delivering deterministic maximum-likelihood estimation without simulation. The exponential special admits a closed-form likelihood function through the lower and upper incomplete gamma functions respectively. The framework extends to inefficiency determinants via a scaling model that preserves the same properties, and is validated through Monte Carlo experiments covering both cases. An empirical application to patent counts illustrates that the model recovers firm-level technical efficiency scores that are robust to the shape of the inefficiency distribution.
keywords: [Stochastic Frontier, Count data, Poisson, Gamma inefficiency, Maximum Likelihood]
---


# Introduction {#sec-introduction}


::: {.cell .hidden layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code .hidden}
library(countSFA)
```
:::


Measuring technical efficiency, the gap between observed output and the
maximum attainable given inputs, is a central objective of production
analysis. Stochastic frontier analysis (SFA), introduced by @aigner1977
and @meeusenBroeck1977, provides the principal econometric framework for
this task. The two founding papers placed one-sided disturbances on the
half-normal and exponential distributions, respectively; @greene1980
extended the framework to maximum-likelihood estimation, and
@greene1990 generalised the inefficiency term to a Gamma distribution,
whose shape parameter accommodates an interior mode rather than a density
pinned at zero. The Gamma generalisation in continuous-output SFA does
not admit a closed-form likelihood. @greene1990 evaluates the required
integrals by Laguerre quadrature, and @greene2003 later proposes a
simulated-likelihood estimator. @greene1990 reports that the
half-normal and exponential restrictions can substantially distort the
distribution of estimated firm inefficiencies, motivating the more
flexible Gamma shape. This literature was developed for continuously distributed
outputs such as log-value-added or log-output and does not extend
directly to settings in which the dependent variable is a non-negative
integer count.

Count-valued production outcomes are common in applied work. Patents
granted to a firm measure innovative output [@griliches1990;
@hallGrilichesHausman1986]; hospital admissions and emergency-department
visits measure healthcare utilisation; accident reports measure safety
performance; and shipment counts measure trade activity. Such data are supported on
$\{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$, exhibit substantial mass at zero, and are typically
right-skewed. Applying a continuous frontier to a log-transformed count
requires ad hoc adjustments, discards the discrete likelihood, and yields
efficiency scores whose statistical properties are difficult to characterise.

@hoflerScrogin2008 and @feHofler2013 take the first steps toward a count-data frontier; @fe2019 surveys the resulting class of discrete-output SFA models. @feHofler2013 specify $Y_i \mid u_i \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda_i e^{\mp u_i})$ with frontier mean $\lambda_i = \exp(\mathbf{x}_i'\boldsymbol{\beta})$ and a half-normal
inefficiency $u_i \geq 0$, treating both the production ($-$) and cost ($+$) orientations in parallel; the companion Stata package `sfcount` [@feHofler2020] facilitates estimation through maximum simulated likelihood
with Halton sequences in both orientations. Subsequent extensions have addressed unobserved heterogeneity through finite mixtures [@drivasEconomidouTsionas2019] and endogeneity of the regressors via copula corrections [@haschkaHerwartz2022], all retaining the half-normal inefficiency and the simulation-based likelihood. That assumption, however, fixes the mode of inefficiency at zero and inherits a symmetric bell shape from the underlying continuous draw. Features better motivated by the continuous literature rather than by count-data applications. The Fé–Hofler framework also lacks a flexible shape parameter analogous to the Gamma generalisation of @greene1990, restricting practitioners to a single inefficiency shape regardless of the empirical context.

We propose a Poisson stochastic frontier in which the inefficiency term follows a Gamma distribution. We treat both production and cost frontiers in parallel through the conditional model

$$
Y_i \mid u_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i \;\sim\;
   \mathrm{Poisson}\!\left(\lambda(\mathbf{x}_i;\,\boldsymbol{\beta})\, e^{\mp u_i}\right),
\qquad
u_i \;\sim\; \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha,\, b),
$$

with shape $\alpha > 0$ and rate $b > 0$, where the upper sign defines the *production frontier* — covering patent counts, hospital discharges, or scientific publications — in which $\lambda_i$ is an upper bound on the conditional mean; and the lower sign defines the *cost frontier*, in which emissions, accidents, or defects are minimised and inefficiency inflates the realised rate. The frontier mean $\lambda(\mathbf{x}_i;\,\boldsymbol{\beta}) > 0$ may take any positive form; for estimation we adopt the canonical log-linear specification
$\lambda(\mathbf{x}_i;\,\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \exp(\mathbf{x}_i'\boldsymbol{\beta})$.

For general $\alpha$ the marginal PMF admits an absolutely convergent alternating-series representation in the production orientation and a one-dimensional integral with super-exponentially damped integrand in
the cost orientation; in either case the conditional mean $\mathrm{E}[e^{-u_i} \mid Y_i = y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i]$ that defines the firm-level technical efficiency score inherits the same structure.

Setting $\alpha = 1$ recovers the exponential inefficiency of @meeusenBroeck1977 and reduces both PMFs to closed-form expressions through the lower and upper incomplete gamma functions respectively, with parallel closed forms for the efficiency score. The exact (or adaptive-quadrature) likelihood evaluation eliminates the need for
maximum simulated likelihood in either orientation. The incompleteness of the gamma function is the algebraic consequence of the one-sided constraint $u_i \geq 0$: relaxing it and integrating over $u_i \in \mathbb{R}$ replaces the incomplete gamma with the complete gamma and recovers the negative binomial, i.e., the standard overdispersion model of @cameronTrivedi2005 [Ch. 20].

Our contributions are fourfold. First, we derive the marginal PMF and conditional efficiency score under Gamma inefficiency (@sec-model), both expressed as an absolutely convergent alternating series in the
production frontier and a one-dimensional integral in the cost frontier. We show that the exponential case reduces them to closed-form expressions through the lower and upper incomplete gamma functions respectively, eliminating the need for maximum simulated likelihood. Second, we develop a practical maximum likelihood estimator (@sec-estimation), and extend the framework to include inefficiency determinants via a scaling model that
preserves the closed-form likelihood. The cost-orientation likelihood relies on adaptive quadrature on the $v = e^{a + u}$ substitution but otherwise uses the same algorithmic apparatus. Third, we validate the estimator in a Monte Carlo study (@sec-simulations) covering both orientations: in the production frontier, bias and root-mean-squared error shrink at the parametric $\sqrt{n}$ rate, the correctly specified
exponential frontier attains nominal 95% coverage from $n = 500$ onward,
and we document a flat-$\alpha$-direction phenomenon under which the
delta-method confidence interval for the Gamma shape parameter
under-covers across all sample sizes — for which we recommend
profile-likelihood intervals; the cost-orientation exponential frontier
exhibits the same $\sqrt{n}$ convergence and near-nominal coverage
despite the per-observation quadrature, and the Poisson GLM intercept
inflation matches the predicted $\alpha\,\log\bigl(b/(b-1)\bigr)$
factor exactly. Fourth, we apply the model to the @hausman1984 patent
panel — the canonical benchmark for count-data econometrics — and show
that the exponential frontier improves substantially over the baseline
Poisson GLM, that capital intensity and science-sector membership are
statistically significant determinants of inefficiency, and that the
estimated firm-level efficiency scores reveal economically meaningful
heterogeneity.

All methods are implemented in the R package `countSFA`
[@countSFApkg], distributed alongside this paper, which contains
closed-form PMF evaluators, maximum likelihood routines, conditional
efficiency scores, and likelihood-ratio and Vuong tests for model
comparison.

The remainder of the paper is organised as follows. @sec-model derives the
marginal PMF and conditional efficiency score for both production and
cost frontiers under Gamma inefficiency, treating the exponential case as
the closed-form $\alpha = 1$ corollary. @sec-existence characterises the
unconditional and conditional moments, identifies the existence regions for
the rate parameter, and develops the scaling extension to observed
inefficiency determinants. @sec-estimation develops the maximum likelihood
estimator, addresses numerical implementation, and discusses
identification. @sec-simulations reports Monte Carlo evidence on
finite-sample performance. @sec-empirical presents the patent application.
@sec-conclusion summarises and outlines extensions to panel data,
zero-inflated counts, and Bayesian estimation.


# The Model {#sec-model}


::: {.cell .hidden layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code .hidden}
library(countSFA)
library(ggplot2)
```
:::


Let $Y_i$ denote a non-negative integer output for unit $i = 1, \ldots, n$, and let $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^k$ be a vector of observed inputs. The frontier specification posits a maximum (or minimum) expected output level $\lambda_i = \lambda(\mathbf{x}_i;\, \boldsymbol{\beta}) > 0$ from which each unit's realised output is displaced by a one-sided random factor. We treat production and cost frontiers in parallel through

$$
Y_i \mid u_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i \;\sim\;
   \mathrm{Poisson}\!\left(\lambda(\mathbf{x}_i;\boldsymbol{\beta})\, e^{\mp u_i}\right),
\qquad u_i \geq 0,
$$ {#eq-conditional}

where the upper sign defines the *production frontier*, where output is maximised and $\lambda_i$ is an upper bound on the conditional mean, and the lower sign defines the *cost frontier*, and $\lambda_i$ is a lower bound that is inflated by inefficiency. Throughout, $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ is the frontier parameter vector, $u_i \geq 0$ is the technical inefficiency, and the triples $(Y_i, u_i, \mathbf{x}_i)$ are independent across units. We write

$$
a_i \;\equiv\; a(\mathbf{x}_i;\,\boldsymbol{\beta})
   \;=\; \log\lambda(\mathbf{x}_i;\,\boldsymbol{\beta})
$$ {#eq-loglink}

for the log-frontier mean. The closed-form derivations that follow require
only that $\lambda(\mathbf{x}_i;\,\boldsymbol{\beta})$ be positive, and they make
no use of any specific functional form. For estimation, simulation, and the
empirical application we adopt the canonical log-linear specification
$\lambda(\mathbf{x}_i;\,\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \exp(\mathbf{x}_i'\boldsymbol{\beta})$,
under which $a_i = \mathbf{x}_i'\boldsymbol{\beta}$. Nonlinear or semiparametric forms are admissible at no cost to the marginal PMF or to the conditional efficiency score.

The specification follows the count-data frontier of @feHofler2013.
In either orientation, however, their half-normal inefficiency leaves the
marginal PMF as a one-dimensional integral that the package evaluates by
maximum simulated likelihood with Halton sequences. The Gamma family adopted
below yields a closed-form alternating series in the production case and a
one-dimensional integral with a uniformly damped integrand in the cost case,
permitting exact likelihood evaluation without simulation.

## Inefficiency Distribution

Following @greene1990 in the continuous frontier literature, we model

$$
u_i \mid \alpha,\, b \;\sim\; \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha,\, b),
\qquad \alpha > 0,\; b > 0,
$$ {#eq-gamma-dist}

with density $f(u;\, \alpha, b) = b^\alpha u^{\alpha - 1} e^{-bu} / \Gamma(\alpha)$
for $u \geq 0$ (rate parameterisation) and moments

$$
\mathrm{E}[u_i] \;=\; \frac{\alpha}{b},
\qquad
\mathrm{Var}[u_i] \;=\; \frac{\alpha}{b^2}.
$$ {#eq-gamma-moments}

The shape parameter $\alpha$ governs the *form* of the inefficiency
distribution independently of its scale. When $\alpha < 1$ the density is
decreasing on $[0, \infty)$ with mode at zero. When $\alpha = 1$ it is exactly
exponential, and we recover a count-data
analogue of that classical specification of @meeusenBroeck1977. When $\alpha > 1$ the density is
hump-shaped with an interior mode at $(\alpha - 1)/b > 0$, implying that very
small inefficiency is no more likely than some positive reference level. The
latter case is economically important: in sectors where all producers face an
irreducible level of inefficiency, e.g., regulatory compliance costs, adjustment
lags, or organisational slack, a distribution with an interior mode more
accurately reflects the data-generating process. The rate $b$ is a scale
parameter: large $b$ concentrates inefficiency near zero so that nearly all
units approach the frontier; small $b$ implies widespread and severe
inefficiency.

## Marginal PMF

::: {#prp-pmf}

## Marginal PMF under Gamma inefficiency

Let $u_i \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha, b)$ as in @eq-gamma-dist. Under
@eq-conditional, the marginal probability mass function of $Y_i$ takes the
following two forms.

*(i) Production frontier* $\bigl(Y_i \mid u_i \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda_i e^{-u_i})\bigr)$.
Marginalising over $u_i$ yields the absolutely convergent alternating-series
representation

$$
\Pr(Y_i = y \mid \mathbf{x}_i,\, \alpha,\, b)
\;=\; \frac{b^\alpha\, e^{a_i y}}{y!}
  \sum_{j=0}^{\infty}
    \frac{(-1)^j\, e^{j a_i}}{j!\;(y + b + j)^\alpha},
$$ {#eq-pmf-gamma}

for all $a_i < \infty$, $b > 0$, $\alpha > 0$, and
$y \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$.

*(ii) Cost frontier* $\bigl(Y_i \mid u_i \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda_i e^{+u_i})\bigr)$.
No analogous closed-form series exists; the marginal PMF is the
one-dimensional integral

$$
\Pr(Y_i = y \mid \mathbf{x}_i,\, \alpha,\, b)
\;=\; \frac{b^\alpha\, e^{a_i y}}{y!\,\Gamma(\alpha)}
  \int_0^\infty u^{\alpha - 1}\,
     \exp\!\bigl((y - b)\,u \,-\, e^{a_i + u}\bigr)\,\mathrm{d}u,
$$ {#eq-pmf-gamma-cost}

whose integrand is super-exponentially damped as $u \to \infty$ and
integrable at $u = 0^+$ for every $\alpha > 0$, so the integral is finite for
all $b > 0$ and can be approximated by adaptive quadrature.
:::

*Proof.* See @sec-app-pmf-proof.

The series in @eq-pmf-gamma converges absolutely because the ratio of
consecutive absolute term sizes is

$$
\frac{|t_{j+1}|}{|t_j|}
= \frac{e^{a_i}}{j+1} \cdot
  \left(\frac{y+b+j}{y+b+j+1}\right)^\alpha
\;\to\; 0 \quad \text{as } j \to \infty.
$$

For any truncation at $J$ terms the tail is bounded by
$|R_J| \leq |t_{J+1}|/(1 - e^{a_i}/(J+2))$ for $J > e^{a_i} - 2$, so the
absolute truncation error is negligible when $K \geq 3 e^{a_i}$.

### The Exponential Corollary {#sec-pmf-exp}

Setting $\alpha = 1$ collapses the alternating series @eq-pmf-gamma to a
closed form involving the lower incomplete gamma function. The Taylor
expansion of $\gamma(s, x) = \int_0^x t^{s-1} e^{-t}\,\mathrm{d}t$ about
$x = 0$ is
$$
\gamma(s,\, x) \;=\; \sum_{j=0}^\infty \frac{(-1)^j x^{s+j}}{j!\,(s+j)},
$$
so evaluating at $s = y + b$ and $x = e^{a_i}$ and multiplying through by
$e^{-b a_i}$ gives
$$
e^{-b a_i}\,\gamma(y+b,\, e^{a_i})
\;=\; e^{a_i y} \sum_{j=0}^\infty \frac{(-1)^j e^{j a_i}}{j!\,(y+b+j)},
$$
so that @eq-pmf-gamma at $\alpha = 1$ becomes a multiple of
$\gamma(y+b,\, e^{a_i})$. The cost case admits a parallel closed form via the
direct change of variable $t = e^{a_i + u}$ in @eq-pmf-gamma-cost at
$\alpha = 1$: this sends $u \in [0, \infty)$ to $t \in [e^{a_i}, \infty)$ and
collapses the integrand to $t^{y - b - 1}\, e^{-t}\, e^{-(y-b)a_i}$, whose
integral is $e^{-(y-b)a_i}\,\Gamma(y - b,\,e^{a_i})$. Collecting prefactors:

::: {#cor-pmf-exp}

## Exponential closed form

When $\alpha = 1$ the marginal PMFs reduce to
$$
\Pr(Y_i = y \mid \mathbf{x}_i,\, b)
\;=\; \frac{b\, e^{-b a_i}}{y!}\;\gamma\!\left(y + b,\; e^{a_i}\right)
$$ {#eq-pmf-exp}

in the production frontier, and to
$$
\Pr(Y_i = y \mid \mathbf{x}_i,\, b)
\;=\; \frac{b\, e^{a_i b}}{y!}\;\Gamma\!\left(y - b,\; e^{a_i}\right)
$$ {#eq-cost-pmf}

in the cost frontier, where $\gamma(s, x) = \int_0^x t^{s-1} e^{-t}\,\mathrm{d}t$
and $\Gamma(s, x) = \int_x^\infty t^{s-1} e^{-t}\,\mathrm{d}t$ denote the
lower and upper incomplete gamma functions respectively (distinguished
throughout by argument count: the single-argument $\Gamma(\alpha)$ is the
complete gamma function).
:::

The two cases are structurally symmetric: the sign of $a_i b$ in the
prefactor flips, and the lower and upper incomplete gamma exchange. Both
functions are available in standard scientific computing libraries.
To our knowledge the cost-frontier closed form
@eq-cost-pmf has not previously been derived for count-data frontiers; in the
half-normal model of @feHofler2013 both orientations require simulated
likelihood, whereas here both admit exact evaluation through standard special
functions.

::: {.remark title="Frontier constraint and the incomplete gamma"}
The incompleteness of the gamma function in @eq-pmf-exp and @eq-cost-pmf is
not a computational device but the algebraic consequence of the one-sided
constraint $u_i \geq 0$. In the production case the change of variable
$s = \lambda_i e^{-u_i}$ runs over $(0, \lambda_i]$, restricting the
resulting integral to the lower incomplete gamma; in the cost case
$t = \lambda_i e^{+u_i}$ runs over $[\lambda_i, \infty)$ and produces the
upper incomplete gamma. Relaxing $u_i \geq 0$ to $u_i \in \mathbb{R}$ would
extend each integral to all of $(0, \infty)$, replacing the incomplete gamma
with the complete $\Gamma(y+b)$, and recovering the negative binomial PMF of
an unconstrained Poisson--Gamma mixture [@cameronTrivedi2005, Ch. 20] — at
the cost of the frontier interpretation. The frontier model is therefore
econometrically distinct from a negative binomial regression precisely
because the gamma function in its likelihood is incomplete.
:::

::: {.remark title="Negative shape arguments in the cost-frontier PMF"}
The upper incomplete gamma
$\Gamma(s, x) = \int_x^\infty t^{s-1} e^{-t}\,\mathrm{d}t$ is well-defined
for any real $s$ whenever $x > 0$: the exponential decay of $e^{-t}$
dominates the polynomial factor $t^{s-1}$ irrespective of the sign of
$s - 1$, and the lower limit $x = e^{a_i} > 0$ keeps the integrand away from
any singularity at the origin. For count data with $y \geq 0$ and $b > 0$ the
argument $y - b$ in @eq-cost-pmf can be negative (e.g.\ $y = 0$, $b = 1$
gives $s = -1$), but $\Gamma(y - b,\, e^{a_i})$ remains finite. Standard
regularised-incomplete-gamma routines distributed with most statistical
software require shape $> 0$; the unnormalised upper incomplete gamma at
arbitrary real shape is available through GSL-compatible numerical libraries
via the standard recurrence and continued-fraction expansions, or one can
evaluate the integral @eq-pmf-gamma-cost directly by quadrature.
:::

@fig-pmf-shape illustrates the marginal PMF in the exponential case across a
range of rate values. As $b$ decreases from a large value toward zero,
probability mass shifts leftward and the distribution becomes progressively
heavier-tailed compared with the Poisson reference, reflecting the increasing
prevalence and severity of inefficiency.


::: {#cell-fig-pmf-shape .cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code .hidden}
a_val <- log(8)   # frontier mean lambda = 8
y_seq <- 0:25

pmf_grid <- do.call(rbind, lapply(c(0.5, 1, 2, 5), function(b_val) {
  pmf_vals <- sapply(y_seq, function(y)
    exp(pmf_poisson_gamma(y, a_val, b = b_val, alpha = 1L))
  )
  data.frame(y = y_seq, pmf = pmf_vals, b = paste0("b = ", b_val))
}))

# Poisson(lambda) reference
pois_ref <- data.frame(
  y   = y_seq,
  pmf = dpois(y_seq, lambda = exp(a_val))
)

ggplot() +
  geom_col(data = pois_ref, aes(x = y, y = pmf),
           fill = "grey80", colour = "grey60", width = 0.8) +
  geom_line(data = pmf_grid,
            aes(x = y, y = pmf, colour = b, linetype = b),
            linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(data = pmf_grid,
             aes(x = y, y = pmf, colour = b, shape = b),
             size = 1.6) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Dark2", name = NULL) +
  scale_linetype_discrete(name = NULL) +
  scale_shape_discrete(name = NULL) +
  labs(x = expression(italic(y)), y = "Probability") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "inside",
        legend.position.inside = c(0.82, 0.72))
```

The grey bars show the Poisson($\lambda_i$) distribution, attained as $b \to \infty$.](index_files/figure-pdf/fig-pmf-shape-1.pdf){#fig-pmf-shape fig-align='center' fig-pos='H' width=75%}
:::
:::


@fig-gamma-pmf shows how the shape parameter $\alpha$ reshapes the same
distribution at fixed scale $b = 1$. As $\alpha$ rises from $0.5$ (decreasing
density) toward $4$ (strongly hump-shaped density), the PMF concentrates: the
probability of very low counts falls because the inefficiency density rules
out near-zero values, and the distribution tightens around its mean.
Conversely $\alpha < 1$ produces a heavier left tail than the exponential, as
the monotone-decreasing density assigns positive probability to arbitrarily
large inefficiency values.


::: {#cell-fig-gamma-pmf .cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code .hidden}
  )
  data.frame(y = y_seq, pmf = pmf_vals,
             alpha = factor(sprintf("alpha == %g", al),
                            levels = sprintf("alpha == %g", alphas)))
}))
```

## Technical Efficiency Scores

Following standard practice in stochastic frontier analysis
[@kumbhakarLovell2000, Ch. 2], the *technical efficiency* of unit $i$ is
defined as the conditional expectation of the efficiency factor $e^{-u_i}$
given the observed count,

$$
\widehat{\mathrm{TE}}_i
\;\equiv\; \mathrm{E}\!\left[e^{-u_i} \mid Y_i = y_i,\; \mathbf{x}_i\right].
$$ {#eq-te-def}

This decomposition separates the one-sided inefficiency from the Poisson
noise using the full likelihood structure [@JLMS:1982].

::: {#prp-te}

## Conditional efficiency score under Gamma inefficiency

Under the Gamma inefficiency assumption @eq-gamma-dist and the conditional
model @eq-conditional, the conditional technical efficiency takes the
following two forms.

*(i) Production frontier.*

$$
\mathrm{E}\!\left[e^{-u_i} \mid Y_i = y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i\right]
\;=\;
\frac{\displaystyle\sum_{j=0}^\infty
        \dfrac{(-1)^j e^{j a_i}}{j!\,(y_i + b + 1 + j)^\alpha}}
     {\displaystyle\sum_{j=0}^\infty
        \dfrac{(-1)^j e^{j a_i}}{j!\,(y_i + b + j)^\alpha}}.
$$ {#eq-te-gamma}

*(ii) Cost frontier.*

$$
\mathrm{E}\!\left[e^{-u_i} \mid Y_i = y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i\right]
\;=\;
\frac{\displaystyle\int_0^\infty u^{\alpha-1}\,
        \exp\!\bigl((y_i - b - 1)\,u - e^{a_i + u}\bigr)\,\mathrm{d}u}
     {\displaystyle\int_0^\infty u^{\alpha-1}\,
        \exp\!\bigl((y_i - b)\,u - e^{a_i + u}\bigr)\,\mathrm{d}u}.
$$ {#eq-te-gamma-cost}

Both integrals are finite for all $b > 0$ and $\alpha > 0$ by the finiteness
argument used for @eq-pmf-gamma-cost; numerical quadrature is required for
the cost case.
:::

*Proof.* See @sec-app-te-proof.

The score lies in $(0, 1)$ for all parameter values: $u_i \geq 0$ implies
$e^{-u_i} \in (0, 1]$ almost surely, so the conditional mean — a convex
combination of such values — is too. The dependence on the data enters
entirely through $y_i$ (the observed count) and $a_i$ (the fitted
log-frontier for unit $i$): a unit with $y_i = 0$ but a large frontier mean
$\lambda_i$ receives a low score in the production case, correctly
diagnosing severe inefficiency; as $b \to \infty$, the score converges
uniformly to $1$ in either orientation.

### The Exponential Corollary {#sec-te-exp}

At $\alpha = 1$ the production series in @eq-te-gamma satisfies
$\sum_{j=0}^\infty (-1)^j e^{ja_i}/[j!(y_i+b+1+j)] = e^{-(y_i+b+1)a_i}\,\gamma(y_i+b+1,\, e^{a_i})$
by the same Taylor identity used in @cor-pmf-exp, and analogously the
denominator equals $e^{-(y_i+b)a_i}\,\gamma(y_i+b,\, e^{a_i})$. The
cost-frontier integrals admit the parallel closed form via the direct
substitution $t = e^{a_i + u}$, which delivers
$\int_0^\infty \exp\bigl((y_i - b - 1)u - e^{a_i + u}\bigr)\,\mathrm{d}u
= e^{-(y_i - b - 1)a_i}\,\Gamma(y_i - b - 1,\, e^{a_i})$
in the numerator and
$e^{-(y_i - b)a_i}\,\Gamma(y_i - b,\, e^{a_i})$ in the denominator.
Cancelling common factors gives:

::: {#cor-te-exp}

## Exponential closed form

When $\alpha = 1$ the conditional efficiency score reduces to
$$
\mathrm{E}\!\left[e^{-u_i} \mid Y_i = y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i\right]
\;=\; e^{-a_i}\cdot
  \frac{\gamma\!\left(y_i + b + 1,\; e^{a_i}\right)}
       {\gamma\!\left(y_i + b,\; e^{a_i}\right)}
$$ {#eq-te-formula}

in the production frontier, and to
$$
\mathrm{E}\!\left[e^{-u_i} \mid Y_i = y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i\right]
\;=\; e^{a_i}\cdot
  \frac{\Gamma\!\left(y_i - b - 1,\; e^{a_i}\right)}
       {\Gamma\!\left(y_i - b,\; e^{a_i}\right)}
$$ {#eq-te-formula-cost}

in the cost frontier.
:::

The structural symmetry of @cor-pmf-exp carries over: the sign on $a_i$ in
the prefactor flips, and the lower and upper incomplete gamma exchange (with
the shape arguments $y_i + b$, $y_i + b + 1$ replaced by $y_i - b$,
$y_i - b - 1$). Both forms require no quadrature.

@fig-te-shape illustrates how the exponential efficiency score rises with
observed output $y_i$ at fixed $a_i$, and how the curvature depends on $b$.


::: {#cell-fig-te-shape .cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code .hidden}
te_grid <- do.call(rbind, lapply(c(0.5, 1, 2, 5), function(b_val) {
  te_vals <- sapply(y_seq, function(y) {
    log_r <- lgamma(y + b_val + 1) - lgamma(y + b_val) +
      pgamma(exp(a_val), shape = y + b_val + 1, rate = 1, log.p = TRUE) -
      pgamma(exp(a_val), shape = y + b_val,     rate = 1, log.p = TRUE)
    exp(-a_val + log_r)
  })
  data.frame(y = y_seq, te = te_vals, b = paste0("b = ", b_val))
}))

ggplot(te_grid, aes(x = y, y = te, colour = b, linetype = b)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.85) +
```

@fig-gamma-te shows how the slope and curvature change with $\alpha$ at
fixed $a_i$ and $b$. When $\alpha > 1$ and $b$ is moderate, the hump-shaped

so the score is uniformly higher at low counts than under the exponential.


::: {#cell-fig-gamma-te .cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code .hidden}
te_series <- function(y, a, b, alpha, delta = 0, K = 150L) {
  kk <- 0:K
  log_abs <- kk * a - lgamma(kk + 1) - alpha * log(y + b + delta + kk)
  pos <- kk %% 2L == 0L
  lse <- function(lv) { m <- max(lv); m + log(sum(exp(lv - m))) }
  lse_p <- lse(log_abs[pos])
  lse_n <- lse(log_abs[!pos])
  if (lse_p <= lse_n) return(NA_real_)
  exp(lse_p + log1p(-exp(lse_n - lse_p)))
}

te_grid_a <- do.call(rbind, lapply(alphas, function(al) {
  te_vals <- sapply(y_seq, function(y) {
    num <- te_series(y, a_val, b = 1, alpha = al, delta = 1)
    den <- te_series(y, a_val, b = 1, alpha = al, delta = 0)
    if (is.na(num) || is.na(den) || den <= 0) return(NA_real_)
    num / den
  })
  data.frame(y = y_seq, te = te_vals,
             alpha = factor(sprintf("alpha == %g", al),
                            levels = sprintf("alpha == %g", alphas)))
}))

ggplot(te_grid_a, aes(x = y, y = te, colour = alpha, linetype = alpha)) +

    y = expression(widehat(TE)[i])
  ) +
```

The next section turns to the unconditional moments and existence regions
implied by these forms — relevant for reporting marginal means under the
cost frontier — and to the scaling extension that lets observed determinants
enter the inefficiency rate $b$ unit-by-unit.


# Moments, Existence, and Inefficiency Determinants {#sec-existence}


::: {.cell .hidden layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code .hidden}
library(countSFA)
library(ggplot2)
library(kableExtra)
options(knitr.table.format = if (knitr::is_latex_output()) "latex" else "html")
```
:::


This section establishes which moments of $Y_i$ exist under the Gamma
frontier, examines the conditions under which the likelihood and the
conditional efficiency score are well-defined, and then extends the model to
allow observed firm characteristics to enter the inefficiency rate.

## Overdispersion and Limiting Behaviour {#sec-moments}

Three claims together determine when the model can be estimated and which
moments of $Y_i$ can be reported. (i) The marginal PMFs @eq-pmf-gamma and
@eq-pmf-gamma-cost — and hence the log-likelihood — are well-defined for all
$b > 0$, with no restriction. (ii) The conditional moments
$\mathrm{E}[e^{r u_i}\mid y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i]$ that underlie the technical
efficiency scores @eq-te-gamma and @eq-te-gamma-cost are finite in *both*
orientations, regardless of $b$. (iii) The unconditional moments of $Y_i$
exist for all $b > 0$ in the production case, but require $b > 1$ for the
mean and $b > 2$ for the variance in the cost case. Only (iii) imposes a
restriction on $b$, and only when reporting the marginal moments — never for
the likelihood, the MLE, or the efficiency scores.

The Gamma MGF evaluated at $t = -1$ and $t = -2$ gives

$$
\mathrm{E}[e^{-u_i}] \;=\; \left(\frac{b}{b+1}\right)^{\!\alpha},
\qquad
\mathrm{E}[e^{-2 u_i}] \;=\; \left(\frac{b}{b+2}\right)^{\!\alpha},
$$ {#eq-mgf-prod}

both arguments negative, so these moments exist for all $b > 0$. Combining
with the conditional Poisson variance via the variance decomposition yields,
in the production frontier,

$$
\mathrm{E}[Y_i]
\;=\; \lambda_i \!\left(\frac{b}{b+1}\right)^{\!\alpha},
\qquad
\mathrm{Var}[Y_i]
\;=\; \mathrm{E}[Y_i]
  + \lambda_i^2\!\left[\!\left(\frac{b}{b+2}\right)^{\!\alpha}
    - \left(\frac{b}{b+1}\right)^{\!2\alpha}\right].
$$ {#eq-moments-gamma}

The variance-to-mean ratio strictly exceeds one at every parameter value:
the bracketed term is positive iff $b(b+1)^2 > b^2(b+2)$, iff
$(b+1)^2 > b(b+2)$, iff $1 > 0$. The Gamma frontier is therefore
unconditionally overdispersed relative to the Poisson, with the additional
variability attributable to heterogeneous inefficiency growing quadratically
in the frontier mean $\lambda_i$. The bracketed term vanishes as
$b \to \infty$ (no inefficiency) or $\alpha \to \infty$ at fixed mean
$\alpha/b$ (degenerate inefficiency). Setting $\alpha = 1$ recovers the
exponential moments

$$
\mathrm{E}[Y_i] \;=\; \lambda_i\,\frac{b}{b+1},
\qquad
\mathrm{Var}[Y_i] \;=\; \lambda_i\,\frac{b}{b+1}
  + \lambda_i^2\,\frac{b}{(b+1)^2(b+2)},
$$ {#eq-moments}

both visible in @fig-pmf-shape as the leftward shift and quadratic
left-tail growth of the distribution as $b$ decreases. The Poisson limit is
likewise visible as the grey reference bars in @fig-pmf-shape and
@fig-gamma-pmf, which the $b = 5$ (resp.\ $\alpha = 4$) curves closely
approximate. As $b \to 0$ the mean inefficiency $\alpha/b$ diverges,
$\mathrm{E}[Y_i] \to 0$, and all units approach zero output — the
degenerate case of complete inefficiency.

In the cost frontier the analogous formulas use the Gamma MGF at *positive*
arguments and acquire a moment-existence restriction:

$$
\mathrm{E}[Y_i] \;=\; \lambda_i\!\left(\frac{b}{b-1}\right)^{\!\alpha}
\quad(\text{requires } b > 1),
\qquad
\mathrm{Var}[Y_i]\quad(\text{requires } b > 2).
$$ {#eq-moments-cost}

The first of these conditions is the one [@feHofler2013, fn. 7] flag for
the half-normal cost frontier. The next subsection shows that this is the
*only* restriction $b > 1$ imposes on the Gamma frontier: the
log-likelihood and the efficiency score remain well-defined for any
$b > 0$.

## PMF, Likelihood, and Conditional Moment Existence

### PMF and log-likelihood

The PMFs @eq-pmf-gamma and @eq-pmf-gamma-cost are well-defined for all
$b > 0$: in each orientation a super-exponential factor dominates the
polynomial growth from $u^{\alpha-1}$, ensuring absolute convergence of
the alternating series (production) or finiteness of the integrand (cost)
with no restriction on $b$. The detailed argument is given in
@sec-app-likelihood. Because the log-likelihood depends only on the PMF,
it inherits well-definedness on the entire interior of the parameter
space. Under the regularity conditions of @neweyMcFadden1994
[Theorems 2.5 and 3.3] — which hold whenever $\alpha$ is bounded away from
zero and $b$ is bounded away from zero and infinity — the MLE is
consistent and $\sqrt{n}$-asymptotically normal. The shape parameter
$\alpha$ may nonetheless be weakly identified in small samples or shallow
panels, a phenomenon documented in @sec-simulations.

### Conditional moments and the efficiency score

The conditional efficiency scores @eq-te-gamma and @eq-te-gamma-cost are
*conditional* moments of $e^{-u_i}$ given $y_i$, not unconditional moments
of the Gamma random variable. Conditioning on $y_i$ inserts a
Poisson-likelihood factor into the posterior density that strictly enlarges
the set of $r$ for which $\mathrm{E}[e^{r u_i}\mid y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i]$ is
finite. The posterior densities are

$$
f(u_i \mid y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i) \;\propto\; u_i^{\alpha - 1}\,
   \exp\!\bigl(-(b + y_i)\,u_i\bigr)\,
   \exp\!\bigl(-\lambda_i e^{-u_i}\bigr),\qquad u_i > 0,
$$ {#eq-post-prod}

in the production frontier, and

$$
f(u_i \mid y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i) \;\propto\; u_i^{\alpha - 1}\,
   \exp\!\bigl((y_i - b)\,u_i\bigr)\,
   \exp\!\bigl(-\lambda_i e^{u_i}\bigr),
$$ {#eq-post-cost}

in the cost frontier. The conditional moment
$\mathrm{E}[e^{r u_i}\mid y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i]$ is finite if and only if
$r < b + y_i$ in the production case — automatic at $r = -1$ for any
$b > 0$ and $y_i \geq 0$ — and is finite for *every* $r \in \mathbb{R}$
in the cost case, regardless of $b$. The cost-frontier efficiency score is therefore
well-defined for all $b > 0$, in marked contrast to the unconditional MGF
$\mathrm{E}[e^{u_i}] = (b/(b-1))^\alpha$ which requires $b > 1$.
@sec-app-condmoments provides the detailed argument: the non-existence of
the unconditional moment when $b \leq 1$ does not propagate to the
conditional, because the Poisson likelihood supplies additional damping in
the right tail of $u_i$.

::: {.remark title="Practical implication"}
Estimation and efficiency scoring are unaffected by the value of
$\hat b$: the log-likelihood and the conditional expectation
$\mathrm{E}[e^{\pm u_i}\mid y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i]$ are finite for every
$b > 0$ in both orientations. The restriction $b > 1$ matters only when
researchers wish to report the cost-frontier marginal mean
$\mathrm{E}[Y_i]$ in closed form.
:::

## Inefficiency Determinants: The Scaling Model {#sec-scaling}

The homogeneous model treats all units as drawing from the same
inefficiency distribution, implying a common mean
$\mathrm{E}(u_i) = \alpha/b$. In practice, firm-level characteristics —
size, age, ownership, industry classification — may systematically shift
the level of inefficiency, and restricting all units to the same
distribution conflates genuine inefficiency with observable heterogeneity.
The scaling (or multiplicative-heteroscedastic) frontier model of
@caudillFord1993, @hadri1999, and @wangSchmidt2002, which generalises the
panel-frontier formulation of @batteseCoelli1995, accommodates such
inefficiency determinants by writing
$u_i = g(z_i, \delta) \cdot u^*$ where
$u^* \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha, b)$ and $z_i$ is a vector of observed
inefficiency drivers with parameter vector $\delta$ to be estimated jointly
with $\boldsymbol{\beta}$. This implies
$u_i \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha, b_i)$ with a unit-specific rate

$$
b_i \;=\; \frac{b}{g(z_i,\, \delta)}.
$$ {#eq-scaling-rate}

The standard choice $g(z_i, \delta) = \exp(z_i'\delta)$ guarantees
$b_i > 0$ for all values of $\delta$ and yields the log-linear
mean-inefficiency structure

$$
b_i \;=\; b\,\exp\!\left(-z_i'\delta\right),
\qquad
\mathrm{E}(u_i) \;=\; \frac{\alpha\,\exp(z_i'\delta)}{b},
$$ {#eq-scaling-bi}

so that $b_i \to \infty$ (full efficiency) as $z_i'\delta \to -\infty$ and
$b_i \to 0$ (complete inefficiency) as $z_i'\delta \to +\infty$.

A useful property of the count-data frontier is that the closed-form
structure of @prp-pmf and @prp-te survives the introduction of scaling
without modification. Because both the production-frontier alternating
series @eq-pmf-gamma and the cost-frontier integral @eq-pmf-gamma-cost
depend on $b$ only as a scalar, they hold observation-by-observation with
$b$ replaced by $b_i$. In the exponential case ($\alpha = 1$) the
production log-likelihood becomes

$$
\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}, b, \delta)
\;=\; \sum_{i=1}^n \Bigl[
  \log b_i - b_i a_i + \log \gamma\!\left(y_i + b_i,\; e^{a_i}\right)
  - \log y_i!
\Bigr],
$$ {#eq-loglik-scaling}

evaluated exactly at every parameter value via the lower incomplete gamma
function. For general $\alpha$ the production log-likelihood retains the
alternating-series form @eq-pmf-gamma with $b \mapsto b_i$ — no simulation
is required — and the cost-frontier log-likelihood retains its
one-dimensional integral form. This is a significant computational
advantage over scaling models for continuous-output stochastic frontiers
[@kumbhakarLovell2000, Ch. 4], where heteroscedastic composed-error
distributions typically require simulation for likelihood evaluation.

The conditional efficiency score inherits the same analytic structure. In
the exponential production case, the same substitution used to prove
@cor-te-exp gives

$$
\mathrm{E}\!\left[e^{-u_i} \mid y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i,\, z_i\right]
\;=\; e^{-a_i} \cdot
  \frac{\gamma\!\left(y_i + b_i + 1,\; e^{a_i}\right)}
       {\gamma\!\left(y_i + b_i,\; e^{a_i}\right)},
$$ {#eq-te-scaling}

identical in form to @eq-te-formula with $b$ replaced by the unit-specific
$b_i = b\,\exp(-z_i'\delta)$. The variables $z_i$ enter the efficiency
score only through this substitution. Analogous expressions hold for the
cost-frontier exponential case (via @eq-te-formula-cost), the
production-frontier Gamma case (via @eq-te-gamma), and the cost-frontier
Gamma case (via @eq-te-gamma-cost), each with $b \mapsto b_i$.

The null hypothesis of no inefficiency determinants is
$H_0{:}\;\delta = 0$, testable via a likelihood ratio test comparing the
scaling and homogeneous models; under $H_0$ the test statistic is
asymptotically $\chi^2$ with $\dim(\delta)$ degrees of freedom.
Identification is cleanest when the variables in $z_i$ are excluded from
$\mathbf{x}_i$: if the same variable enters both the frontier mean and the
scaling function, identification of $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ and $\delta$
relies on the nonlinear entry of $b_i$ into the PMF rather than on an
exclusion restriction. A natural separation for patent data is to use
$\mathbf{x}_i$ for innovation inputs (R&D, capital) and $z_i$ for
organisational characteristics (firm age, ownership type, industry
indicators) that plausibly affect the *level* of inefficiency without
directly entering the production frontier.

With the PMF, efficiency score, moments, and scaling extension all in hand,
@sec-estimation builds the log-likelihood and describes how numerical
optimisation is conducted, including the treatment of starting values and
the avoidance of degenerate parameter paths on the boundary of the
parameter space.


# Maximum Likelihood Estimation {#sec-estimation}


::: {.cell .hidden layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code .hidden}
library(countSFA)
library(ggplot2)
```
:::


## Log-Likelihood

Let $\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\boldsymbol{\beta}', b, \alpha)'$ collect all
free parameters. Given an independent sample $(y_1, \mathbf{x}_1), \ldots, (y_n, \mathbf{x}_n)$
the log-likelihood takes the additive form

$$
\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta})
\;=\; \sum_{i=1}^n \log \Pr(Y_i = y_i \mid \mathbf{x}_i,\, \boldsymbol{\theta}),
$$ {#eq-loglik}

where the summands are evaluated via @eq-pmf-exp (exponential case, $\alpha = 1$)
or @eq-pmf-gamma (general Gamma). Both PMFs are strictly positive and
log-concave in $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ for fixed $(b, \alpha)$, which makes the
profile over $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ well-behaved. The joint surface over all
parameters is not globally concave, however, so initialisation and
reparametrisation are required.

The parameter space is $\boldsymbol{\beta} \in \mathbb{R}^k$, $b > 0$, $\alpha > 0$.
To impose positivity of $b$ and $\alpha$ without constrained optimisation, we
optimise over the unconstrained reparametrisation

$$
\boldsymbol{\vartheta}
\;=\; \bigl(\boldsymbol{\beta}',\; \log b,\; \log \alpha\bigr)',
$$ {#eq-reparam}

with $\boldsymbol{\vartheta} \in \mathbb{R}^{k+2}$ for the Gamma model and
$\boldsymbol{\vartheta} \in \mathbb{R}^{k+1}$ for the exponential ($\alpha$ fixed
at 1). The objective passed to the numerical optimiser is thus the negative
log-likelihood $-\ell(\boldsymbol{\vartheta})$, to be minimised.

::: {.remark title="Series truncation (production orientation)"}
The PMF in @eq-pmf-gamma is evaluated by truncating the alternating series
at $K = 1000$ terms. The ratio-test bound from @sec-model implies that
$K \approx 3 e^{a_i}$ terms suffice for the absolute truncation error to
fall below machine epsilon at typical frontier means; the implementation
issues a warning if relative convergence is not achieved.
:::

::: {.remark title="Cost-frontier likelihood evaluation"}
The cost-orientation PMF @eq-pmf-gamma-cost admits no closed-form
alternating series (@prp-pmf, part ii). For $\alpha \neq 1$, or for
$\alpha = 1$ with $y_i \leq b$, each per-observation log-PMF is evaluated
by adaptive quadrature on the change of variable $v = e^{a_i + u}$, which
sends @eq-pmf-gamma-cost to a constant times
$$
\int_{e^{a_i}}^{\infty}
   (\log v - a_i)^{\alpha - 1}\, v^{\,y_i - b - 1}\, e^{-v}\,\mathrm{d}v.
$$ {#eq-cost-quad}
The integral is evaluated numerically by adaptive quadrature. At $\alpha = 1$ with $y_i > b$ the closed form @eq-cost-pmf is used directly; for $y_i \leq b$, we revert to numerical integration. Estimation is correspondingly slower in the cost-frontier case.
:::

## Starting Values

Starting values are essential because the likelihood surface has a degenerate ridge and the dimension of the parameter space is $k + 1$ or $k + 2$. We exploit the fact that the Poisson GLM, which ignores
inefficiency, provides consistent estimates of $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ only up to an additive shift in the intercept, but yields consistent estimates of the slope coefficients. Formally, let $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\text{Pois}}$
denote the standard Poisson MLE. Under the homogeneous DGP, where
$u_i \perp \mathbf{x}_i$,
$\log\mathrm{E}[Y_i \mid \mathbf{x}_i] = a_i + \log\mathrm{E}[e^{\mp u_i}]$, so
the slope coefficients are asymptotically unaffected by ignoring $u_i$ while
the intercept is biased by $\alpha\,\log\bigl(b/(b+1)\bigr) < 0$ in the
production orientation and by $\alpha\,\log\bigl(b/(b-1)\bigr) > 0$ in the
cost orientation (the latter requires $b_0 > 1$ for the marginal mean to
exist). The bias direction reverses with the orientation but its
absolute magnitude is the same log-mean-inefficiency factor.

The starting value for $b$ is obtained from the excess Poisson dispersion.
At $\alpha = 1$, @eq-moments gives
$\mathrm{Var}[Y_i] - \mathrm{E}[Y_i] = \lambda_i^2 \cdot b/[(b+1)^2(b+2)]$.
We use the initialisation

$$
b_0 \;=\; \frac{\overline{\hat\mu}^2}{\max\bigl(\mathrm{Var}(Y) - \bar{Y},\, \varepsilon\bigr)},
$$ {#eq-starting-b}

where $\hat\mu_i$ are the Poisson fitted values and $\varepsilon > 0$ is a
small floor preventing division by near-zero excess variance. This is a
heuristic — it inverts the simpler relation
$\mathrm{Var}[Y] \approx \mathrm{E}[Y] + \mathrm{E}[\mu]^2/b$ rather than
the exact moment condition above — and serves only as a starting point;
the optimiser corrects it within a few iterations. The starting value for
$\alpha$ is $\alpha_0 = 1$ (i.e., $\log\alpha_0 = 0$), placing the search at
the exponential sub-model and letting the optimiser find the shape.

The sample-dispersion inversion @eq-starting-b is derived from the
production-orientation moments @eq-moments, which exist for all $b > 0$.
In the cost orientation the corresponding marginal moments
$\mathrm{E}[Y_i]$ and $\mathrm{Var}[Y_i]$ exist only for $b > 1$ and
$b > 2$ respectively (@eq-moments-cost), so the sample mean and variance
of $Y_i$ have no finite probability limit when the true $b$ lies in
$(0, 2]$ and the moment-based inversion lacks an asymptotic justification.
We therefore fix the cost-orientation starter at $b_0 = e^{0.92} \approx 2.5$
— interior to the search interval and above the variance-existence
threshold — and rely on the warm-start from the exponential fit, together
with bound-constrained refinement, to navigate the cost-frontier
likelihood. The $\alpha_0 = 1$ exponential starting point
is unchanged.

## Numerical Optimisation

**Exponential model.** When $\alpha$ is fixed at 1, the $k + 1$ free parameters are $(\boldsymbol{\beta}', \log b)'$. We maximize the log-likelihood using a gradient descent algorithm with a numerical Hessian computed at the solution.

**Gamma model.** Estimating $\alpha$ freely introduces a qualitatively different optimization challenge. The likelihood surface has a nearly flat ridge along the curve $\alpha \to 0$, $b \to \infty$ while holding $\alpha/b$ constant: as $\alpha \to 0$ the Gamma degenerates toward a point mass at zero, reducing the frontier model to a standard Poisson regression. This ridge does not correspond to a local maximum of practical interest but can trap gradient-based methods if the initial value is poorly chosen.

We address this with three strategies. First, we use a *warm start*: the exponential model is fitted first, and its solution $(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}},\log \hat{b})$ together with $\log \alpha_0 = 0$ provides the starting vector
for the Gamma optimisation. This anchors the search near the exponential sub-model, where the likelihood is concave and the gradient is well-defined.

Second, we restrict $\log b \in [-8, 8]$ (corresponding to $b \in [3 \times 10^{-4},\,
3 \times 10^3]$) and $\log \alpha \in [-2, 4]$ (corresponding to
$\alpha \in [0.14,\, 55]$), keeping the optimiser away from the degenerate
boundary. Third, if the optimization algorithm does not converge, a secondary
grid of four alternative $\log \alpha$ starting points is tried and the best
objective value is retained.

The same numerical approach applies to the cost orientation. The per-evaluation cost is set by the quadrature route described in the cost-frontier likelihood remark above rather than by the alternating-series sum.

::: {.remark title="The degeneracy ridge"}
The ridge $\alpha \to 0$, $b \to \infty$ at constant $\alpha/b$ is not an identification failure of the model but a
near-boundary behaviour of the likelihood. At the true $(\alpha_0, b_0)$ with
$\alpha_0 > 0$ finite, the Fisher information matrix is positive definite and
the MLE is consistent and asymptotically normal. The issue is purely numerical:
for any finite sample the gradient of the log-likelihood may be very shallow
near the boundary, making unconstrained optimisation slow or unreliable. The
parameter bounds above exclude the problematic region while leaving any interior
maximum accessible.
:::

@fig-profile-lik illustrates the profile log-likelihood for $\alpha$ on
two simulated DGPs at three sample sizes $n \in \{500, 1000, 2500\}$.
For each DGP a single master dataset of size $n_{\max} = 2500$ is
generated, and the profile at each smaller $n$ uses the first $n$
observations of that same master dataset; the curves within a panel
therefore differ only in the amount of information available, not in
the underlying draws. When the true DGP is exponential
($\alpha_0 = 1$, left panel) the profile is sharply maximised at the
truth already at $n = 500$, and the LR-95% interval narrows
monotonically as $n$ grows. When the true DGP is Gamma with
$\alpha_0 = 2$ and $b_0 = 1$ (right panel) the profile is essentially
flat at $n = 500$, becomes resolved near $\alpha_0$ at $n = 1000$, and
yields a tight interval around the truth at $n = 2500$. The weak
identification of $\alpha$ is a finite-sample phenomenon under high
inefficiency-to-signal ratios, not a structural failure: at
$n = 2500$ the Fisher information in the $\alpha$ direction is large
enough for the profile to recover the truth in either DGP.


::: {#cell-fig-profile-lik .cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code .hidden}
set.seed(20240315L)
n_max  <- 2500L
n_grid <- c(500L, 1000L, n_max)

# For each DGP, draw one master dataset of size n_max once. Profiles at
# smaller n use the first n observations from the same master dataset, so
# the sharpening of the profile as n grows reflects added information
# rather than different draws.
master_dataset <- function(alpha_true, b_true) {
  z   <- rnorm(n_max)
  u   <- rgamma(n_max, shape = alpha_true, rate = b_true)
  lam <- exp(1 + 0.5 * z - u)
  y   <- rpois(n_max, lam)
  list(z = z, y = y)
}

# Profile log-likelihood for alpha on the first n_use observations of `data`.
# At each fixed alpha on the grid the inner optimiser profiles out
# (beta, log b) starting from a warm-start chain walked outward from the
# truth, with a cold-start fallback to detect basin drift.
profile_loglik_on <- function(data, n_use, alpha_true) {
  z <- data$z[seq_len(n_use)]
  y <- data$y[seq_len(n_use)]
  X <- cbind(1, z)

  alpha_grid <- seq(0.5, 6, by = 0.25)
  init_par   <- c(0, 0.5, 0)
  lower      <- c(rep(-5, ncol(X)), -4)
  upper      <- c(rep( 5, ncol(X)),  4)
  K_inner    <- 1000L

  safe_nll <- function(par, y, X, alpha) {
    v <- suppressWarnings(log_lik_poisson_frontier(par, y, X, alpha, K = K_inner))
    if (!is.finite(v) || v > 1e10 || v < 0) 1e10 else v
  }

  fit_at <- function(al, par_init) {
    par_init <- pmin(pmax(par_init, lower + 1e-6), upper - 1e-6)
    optim(par_init, fn = safe_nll,
          y = y, X = X, alpha = al,
          method  = "L-BFGS-B",
          lower   = lower, upper = upper,
          control = list(maxit = 2000, factr = 1e2))
  }

  ord  <- order(abs(alpha_grid - alpha_true))
  ll   <- numeric(length(alpha_grid))
  pars <- vector("list", length(alpha_grid))
  for (k in seq_along(ord)) {
    idx    <- ord[k]
    fitted <- which(!vapply(pars, is.null, logical(1)))
    warm   <- if (length(fitted) == 0L) init_par else
      pars[[fitted[which.min(abs(alpha_grid[fitted] - alpha_grid[idx]))]]]
    cands <- list(
      tryCatch(fit_at(alpha_grid[idx], warm),     error = function(e) NULL),
      tryCatch(fit_at(alpha_grid[idx], init_par), error = function(e) NULL)
    )
    cands <- Filter(function(o) !is.null(o) && is.finite(o$value), cands)
    if (length(cands) == 0L) {
      ll[idx]     <- NA_real_
      pars[[idx]] <- warm
    } else {
      best <- cands[[which.min(vapply(cands, `[[`, numeric(1L), "value"))]]
      ll[idx]     <- if (best$value < 1e9) -best$value else NA_real_
      pars[[idx]] <- best$par
    }
  }
  data.frame(alpha = alpha_grid, loglik = ll,
             dgp   = paste0("True \u03b1\u2080 = ", alpha_true),
             n     = n_use)
}

# Build the full (DGP, n) profile grid using nested master datasets.
prof_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(c(1, 2), function(at) {
  dat <- master_dataset(alpha_true = at, b_true = 1)
  do.call(rbind, lapply(n_grid, function(nn)
    profile_loglik_on(dat, nn, alpha_true = at)))
}))
prof_df <- prof_df[is.finite(prof_df$loglik), ]

# Centre each (DGP, n) curve at its maximum so the LR threshold reads
# directly off the y-axis.
prof_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(
  split(prof_df, list(prof_df$dgp, prof_df$n), drop = TRUE),
  function(d) {
    d$loglik_c <- d$loglik - max(d$loglik, na.rm = TRUE)
    d
  }))

prof_df$n_lab <- factor(paste0("n = ", format(prof_df$n, big.mark = ",")),
                        levels = paste0("n = ", format(n_grid, big.mark = ",")))

ggplot(prof_df, aes(x = alpha, y = loglik_c,
                    colour = n_lab, linetype = n_lab, group = n_lab)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 0.9) +
  geom_vline(data = data.frame(
               dgp  = c("True \u03b1\u2080 = 1", "True \u03b1\u2080 = 2"),
               xint = c(1, 2)),
             aes(xintercept = xint),
             linetype = "dashed", colour = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.6,
             inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = -qchisq(0.95, df = 1) / 2,
             linetype = "dotted", colour = "grey40") +
  facet_wrap(~ dgp) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("#9ecae1", "#3182bd", "#08306b"),
                      name = NULL) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("dotted", "dashed", "solid"),
                        name = NULL) +
  labs(x = expression(alpha), y = "Profile log-likelihood (centered)") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The horizontal dotted line in @fig-profile-lik marks the 95% confidence
threshold $-\tfrac{1}{2}\chi^2_{1,0.95} \approx -1.92$: values of
$\alpha$ whose profile log-likelihood exceeds this threshold form a
likelihood-ratio confidence interval for $\alpha$. Reading the figure
along the $n$-axis confirms that weak $\alpha$ identification is a
finite-sample, high-inefficiency phenomenon rather than a structural
defect of the model. In the exponential DGP the profile is already
sharply peaked at $n = 500$ and tightens further with $n$. In the
Gamma DGP, the $n = 500$ profile is flat across nearly the full
displayed grid, the $n = 1000$ profile begins to resolve, and by
$n = 2500$ the LR-95% interval has narrowed substantially around the
truth. The practitioner remark in this section recommends
profile-likelihood inference whenever $n$ is in the range where the
Wald approximation around $\hat\alpha$ remains unreliable.

## Standard Errors and Inference

Standard errors for all parameters are derived from the numerical Hessian
$\widehat{\mathbf{H}} = -\partial^2 \ell / \partial \boldsymbol{\vartheta}\,
\partial \boldsymbol{\vartheta}'$ evaluated at the MLE. The inverse
$\widehat{\mathbf{V}} = \widehat{\mathbf{H}}^{-1}$ provides the asymptotic
variance of $\hat{\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}$. Since $b$ and $\alpha$ are
estimated on the log scale, standard errors on the natural scale are obtained
via the delta method:

$$
\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}(\hat b)
\;=\; \hat b \cdot \widehat{\mathrm{SE}}(\log \hat b),
\qquad
\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}(\hat\alpha)
\;=\; \hat\alpha \cdot \widehat{\mathrm{SE}}(\log \hat\alpha).
$$ {#eq-delta-se}

The slope coefficients $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ enter linearly, so their standard
errors are read directly from the corresponding diagonal elements of
$\widehat{\mathbf{V}}$.

Under standard regularity conditions — the true parameter vector is in the
interior of the parameter space, the log-likelihood is twice continuously
differentiable, and the information matrix is non-singular — the MLE is
consistent and satisfies

$$
\sqrt{n}\,\bigl(\hat{\boldsymbol{\vartheta}} - \boldsymbol{\vartheta}_0\bigr)
\;\xrightarrow{d}\; \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mathbf{0},\;
  \mathbf{I}(\boldsymbol{\vartheta}_0)^{-1}\right),
$$ {#eq-asymp-normal}

where $\mathbf{I}(\boldsymbol{\vartheta}_0)$ is the Fisher information matrix.
These conditions hold at any interior point of the parameter space; the Monte
Carlo experiment of @sec-simulations confirms that the normal approximation is
accurate for $n \geq 500$ across all DGP configurations considered.

::: {.remark title="Weak identification of $\alpha$"}
In practice, $\alpha$ may be weakly identified: the shape of the inefficiency
distribution is determined by the curvature of the log-likelihood in the
$\alpha$ direction, which is often flat relative to the curvature in the
$(\boldsymbol{\beta}, b)$ directions. The Monte Carlo results in
@sec-simulations document this. Although the bias and RMSE of $\hat\alpha$
shrink at the parametric $\sqrt{n}$ rate, the delta-method 95% CI for $\alpha$
under-covers across the entire grid $n \in \{500, 1000, 2500\}$ — the
observed-information Hessian under-states the joint variability of
$(\hat\alpha, \hat b)$ when the $\alpha$-ridge is shallow. We therefore
recommend reporting profile-likelihood intervals for $\alpha$
(@fig-profile-lik) rather than relying solely on the delta-method Wald
interval whenever the shape parameter is of inferential interest.
:::


## Hypothesis Testing

The likelihood ratio (LR) test compares the exponential and Gamma
frontier models. The test statistic

$$
\Lambda
\;=\; 2\bigl[\ell(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\text{Gamma}})
            - \ell(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\text{Exp}})\bigr]
\;\xrightarrow{d}\; \chi^2_1
\quad \text{under } H_0{:}\ \alpha = 1
$$ {#eq-lr-test}

follows a chi-squared distribution with one degree of freedom (the single
restriction $\alpha = 1$), provided the exponential model is estimated
at the same $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ and $b$ values — which is guaranteed because
the exponential is the value of the Gamma log-likelihood evaluated at
$\alpha = 1$ and the jointly optimised $(\boldsymbol{\beta}, b)$.

::: {.remark title="Boundary test"}
The standard regularity condition for the $\chi^2_1$ limit in @eq-lr-test requires that $\alpha_0 = 1$ lies in the *interior* of the parameter space for $\alpha$. Since $\alpha$ is bounded below at zero, $\alpha_0 = 1$ is interior, and the chi-squared reference distribution applies. A more delicate boundary
issue would arise if we tested $H_0: \alpha = 0$ (which would put $\alpha_0$ on the boundary), but we do not consider that degenerate case.
:::

Wald tests for individual parameters follow from @eq-asymp-normal and
@eq-delta-se in the usual way. For the shape parameter specifically, the null
$H_0: \alpha = 1$ can be tested via either the LR statistic @eq-lr-test or
the Wald statistic $(\hat\alpha - 1)^2 / \widehat{\mathrm{Var}}(\hat\alpha)$;
the LR form is generally preferred for its better finite-sample size control
[@cameronTrivedi2005, Ch. 7].

## Computing Efficiency Scores

After estimation, firm-level technical efficiency scores are computed as the
conditional expectation $\widehat{\mathrm{TE}}_i = \mathrm{E}[e^{-u_i} \mid
y_i, \mathbf{x}_i]$ described in @sec-model. The orientation stored on the
fitted object selects the formula. At $\alpha = 1$ the closed
forms are used: @eq-te-formula in the production frontier, and
@eq-te-formula-cost in the cost frontier whenever the shape arguments
$y_i - b$ and $y_i - b - 1$ are both positive (i.e.\ $y_i > b + 1$). For
general $\alpha$, or for $\alpha = 1$ in the cost orientation when
$y_i \leq b + 1$ falls into the negative-shape regime where the
closed-form expression is unavailable, the conditional mean and second
moment are obtained by
numerical integration of the conditional density kernel — the $u$-form
@eq-post-prod for production,

$$
h(u; y_i, a_i)
\;\propto\; \exp\!\bigl(-e^{a_i - u} - (y_i + b)\,u\bigr)\, u^{\alpha-1},
\qquad u > 0,
$$ {#eq-post-kernel}

with integration grid centred at the production-kernel mode
$\tilde{u}_i = a_i - \log(y_i + b)$, and the $v = e^{a_i + u}$ transform of
@eq-post-cost for cost (the same single-exponential damping used in the
cost-PMF integral @eq-cost-quad), with grid centred at
$v_i^* = \max(e^{a_i},\, y_i - b)$ and width set by an approximating Gamma
standard deviation $\sqrt{\max(y_i - b,\,1) + \alpha}$. An approximate 95%
conditional interval is constructed as
$\widehat{\mathrm{TE}}_i \pm 1.96\,\widehat{\sigma}_i$, where
$\widehat{\sigma}_i^2 = \mathrm{E}[e^{-2u_i} \mid y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i] - \widehat{\mathrm{TE}}_i^2$
is estimated from the same integration. In the empirical application (patent
data, $n = 1730$), the closed-form series suffers catastrophic cancellation for
observations with $y_i > 100$ and $\exp(\hat{a}_i) > 200$; the quadrature-based
PMF evaluator described above is used as a fallback in these cases, with
accuracy verified to exceed $10^{-4}$ relative tolerance against
high-precision adaptive quadrature.

With the estimation framework established, @sec-simulations examines
finite-sample performance of the MLE across twelve DGP configurations that
vary the true $\alpha_0$, $b_0$, and sample size $n$.

```{.r .cell-code .hidden}
mc       <- readRDS("code/simulations/results.rds")
mc_wide  <- mc$mc_wide
dgp_grid <- mc$dgp_grid

# Re-summarise with a robustness filter that drops replications whose point
# estimates or standard errors blow up despite optim returning a "converged"
# code. This catches the flat alpha → 0, b → infty ridge that the L-BFGS-B
# bounds in fit_poisson_frontier are designed to avoid but cannot rule out
# entirely in finite samples (cf. the weak-identification warning in §4).
robust_stats <- function(est, se, true_val,
                         est_max = 1e3, se_max = 1e3) {
  ok <- is.finite(est) & is.finite(se) &
        abs(est) < est_max & abs(se) < se_max
  if (!any(ok))
    return(data.frame(bias = NA_real_, rmse = NA_real_,
                      coverage = NA_real_, conv_rate = 0))
  err   <- est[ok] - true_val
  ci_lo <- est[ok] - 1.96 * se[ok]
  ci_hi <- est[ok] + 1.96 * se[ok]
  data.frame(
    bias      = mean(err),
    rmse      = sqrt(mean(err^2)),
    coverage  = mean(ci_lo <= true_val & true_val <= ci_hi),
    conv_rate = mean(ok)
  )
}

summary_rows <- list()
for (g in seq_len(nrow(dgp_grid))) {
  a_t <- dgp_grid$alpha_true[g]
  b_t <- dgp_grid$b_true[g]
  n_g <- dgp_grid$n[g]
  sub <- mc_wide[mc_wide$alpha_true == a_t &
                   mc_wide$b_true == b_t &
                   mc_wide$n == n_g, ]

  add <- function(model, param, est, se, true_val) {
    s <- robust_stats(est, se, true_val)
    summary_rows[[length(summary_rows) + 1L]] <<- data.frame(
      alpha_true = a_t, b_true = b_t, n = n_g,
      model = model, param = param, true_val = true_val,
      s, stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    )
  }

  add("Poisson", "beta1", sub$b1_pois, sub$se1_pois, 1)
  add("Poisson", "beta2", sub$b2_pois, sub$se2_pois, 0.5)

  ok_e <- sub$conv_exp == 0L
  add("Exp", "beta1", sub$b1_exp[ok_e], sub$se1_exp[ok_e], 1)
  add("Exp", "beta2", sub$b2_exp[ok_e], sub$se2_exp[ok_e], 0.5)
  add("Exp", "b",     sub$b_exp [ok_e], sub$seb_exp[ok_e], b_t)

  ok_g <- sub$conv_gam == 0L
  add("Gamma", "beta1", sub$b1_gam[ok_g], sub$se1_gam [ok_g], 1)
  add("Gamma", "beta2", sub$b2_gam[ok_g], sub$se2_gam [ok_g], 0.5)
  add("Gamma", "b",     sub$b_gam [ok_g], sub$seb_gam [ok_g], b_t)
  add("Gamma", "alpha", sub$al_gam[ok_g], sub$seal_gam[ok_g], a_t)

  for (mod in c("exp", "gam")) {
    col    <- if (mod == "exp") sub$mae_exp else sub$mae_gam
    ok_col <- sub[[paste0("conv_", mod)]] == 0L
    summary_rows[[length(summary_rows) + 1L]] <- data.frame(
      alpha_true = a_t, b_true = b_t, n = n_g,
      model = if (mod == "exp") "Exp" else "Gamma",
      param = "TE_MAE", true_val = 0,
      bias       = mean(col[ok_col], na.rm = TRUE),
      rmse       = sd  (col[ok_col], na.rm = TRUE),
      coverage   = NA_real_,
      conv_rate  = mean(ok_col),
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    )
  }
}
mc_summary <- do.call(rbind, summary_rows)
rownames(mc_summary) <- NULL
```

## Experimental Design

We validate the maximum likelihood estimators developed in @sec-estimation
through a controlled Monte Carlo experiment. The data-generating process
mirrors the model of @sec-model exactly:

$$
Y_i \mid u_i \;\sim\; \mathrm{Poisson}\!\left(\exp(\mathbf{x}_i'\boldsymbol{\beta}_0 - u_i)\right),
\qquad u_i \;\sim\; \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha_0,\, b_0),
$$ {#eq-dgp}

with $\mathbf{x}_i = (1,\, z_i)'$, $z_i \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} N(0, 1)$,
and true frontier coefficients $\boldsymbol{\beta}_0 = (1,\; 0.5)'$.
The inefficiency distribution is varied across a $2 \times 2$ grid:
$\alpha_0 \in \{1, 2\}$ (exponential versus hump-shaped Gamma density) and
$b_0 \in \{1, 2\}$ (lower versus higher average inefficiency,
$\bar{u}_0 = \alpha_0/b_0 \in \{0.5, 1, 2\}$). Three sample sizes
$n \in \{500, 1000, 2500\}$ are crossed with this grid, yielding twelve
experimental cells and $R = 1{,}000$ independent replications each. Seeds
are pre-drawn from a fixed master seed to ensure exact reproducibility under
any parallel schedule.

Three estimators are evaluated in every replication. The *Poisson GLM* fits
a standard Poisson regression that ignores inefficiency entirely, serving as
the misspecified benchmark. It consistently estimates $\log \mathrm{E}[Y_i \mid \mathbf{x}_i]
= a_i + \log \mathrm{E}[e^{-u_i}]$, so slope coefficients are
asymptotically unbiased but the intercept is attenuated by
$\alpha_0 \log\bigl(b_0/(b_0+1)\bigr)$ (cf.\ @eq-pois-attenuation). The *Exp frontier* fits the
Poisson frontier with exponential inefficiency ($\alpha = 1$ fixed by maximum
likelihood); it is correctly specified when $\alpha_0 = 1$ and misspecified
otherwise. The *Gamma frontier* estimates both $\alpha$ and $b$ freely and
is correctly specified across all twelve cells.

A natural additional competitor is the negative binomial GLM — the limiting
case of the frontier model as the inefficiency constraint $u_i \geq 0$ is
dropped — but because it conflates frontier and random-effects heterogeneity
it is omitted from the main comparisons; its inclusion is left for future work.

For each fitted model we record the MLE of all free parameters, their
delta-method standard errors (as described in @sec-estimation), convergence
status, whether each nominal 95% confidence interval covers the true value,
and the mean absolute error (MAE) of the estimated technical efficiency
scores against the oracle scores $e^{-u_i}$ drawn from the DGP.

::: {.remark title="Oracle efficiency scores"}
The oracle score $e^{-u_i}$ is the
exact realised efficiency of unit $i$ in a given replication — a quantity that
is unobservable in practice but known in simulation. The MAE
$n^{-1}\sum_i |\hat{\mathrm{TE}}_i - e^{-u_i}|$ measures how closely the
conditional mean efficiency approximates the truth, integrating both estimation
error in $(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}, \hat b, \hat\alpha)$ and the irreducible
noise from the conditional averaging over $u_i$.
:::

All PMF evaluations and efficiency score computations rely solely on
standard special functions: the exponential case uses the regularised
lower incomplete gamma function, while the general Gamma case applies a
signed log-sum-exp algorithm to the alternating series @eq-pmf-gamma. The
implementation switches to the closed-form $\alpha = 1$ path whenever
$|\alpha - 1| < 10^{-10}$, ensuring numerical stability at the exponential
boundary. The full implementation will be distributed in an accompanying R
package.

## Parameter Recovery

@tbl-sim-bias reports bias and root mean squared error (RMSE) for the frontier
coefficients $\beta_1$ (intercept) and $\beta_2$ (slope) together with the
inefficiency rate $b$, at two representative DGP cells: $(\alpha_0, b_0) = (1, 1)$,
where the exponential frontier is correctly specified, and $(\alpha_0, b_0) = (2, 1)$,
where only the Gamma frontier is correctly specified.


::: {#tbl-sim-bias .cell layout-align="center" tbl-cap='Bias and RMSE of three estimators across sample sizes. Upper panel: DGP $(\alpha_0, b_0) = (1,1)$ (exponential inefficiency). Lower panel: DGP $(\alpha_0, b_0) = (2,1)$ (Gamma inefficiency). True values: $\beta_1 = 1$, $\beta_2 = 0.5$, $b = b_0$. Sample sizes: $n \in \{500, 1000, 2500\}$. The Poisson GLM has no $b$ or $\alpha$ parameter (shown as ---).'}

```{.r .cell-code .hidden}
fmt <- function(x) ifelse(is.na(x), "---", sprintf("%.3f", x))

build_row <- function(a0, b0, n_val, param) {
  get_br <- function(mod) {
    r <- mc_summary[mc_summary$alpha_true == a0 &
                    mc_summary$b_true    == b0 &
                    mc_summary$n         == n_val &
                    mc_summary$model     == mod &
                    mc_summary$param     == param, ]
    if (nrow(r) == 0) return(c("---", "---"))
    c(fmt(r$bias), fmt(r$rmse))
  }
  pois <- get_br("Poisson")
  exp_ <- get_br("Exp")
  gam  <- get_br("Gamma")
  data.frame(
    `$n$`          = n_val,
    Parameter      = switch(param,
      beta1 = "$\\beta_1$", beta2 = "$\\beta_2$",
      b = "$b$", alpha = "$\\alpha$"),
    `Bias (Pois)`  = pois[1], `RMSE (Pois)` = pois[2],
    `Bias (Exp)`   = exp_[1], `RMSE (Exp)`  = exp_[2],
    `Bias (Gamma)` = gam[1],  `RMSE (Gamma)`= gam[2],
    check.names = FALSE, stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
}

panels <- list()
for (dgp in list(c(1, 1), c(2, 1))) {
  a0 <- dgp[1]; b0 <- dgp[2]
  rows <- lapply(
    list(c(500,  "beta1"), c(500,  "beta2"), c(500,  "b"),
         c(1000, "beta1"), c(1000, "beta2"), c(1000, "b"),
         c(2500, "beta1"), c(2500, "beta2"), c(2500, "b")),
    function(x) build_row(a0, b0, as.integer(x[1]), x[2])
  )
  panels[[length(panels) + 1]] <- do.call(rbind, rows)
}
bias_tbl <- do.call(rbind, panels)

  add_header_above(c(" " = 2,
```

Several patterns emerge from @tbl-sim-bias. The Poisson GLM produces
substantial downward bias in $\beta_1$ across all cells. From
$\log\mathrm{E}[Y_i\mid\mathbf{x}_i] = a_i + \log\mathrm{E}[e^{-u_i}]$ and
$\mathrm{E}[e^{-u_i}] = (b_0/(b_0+1))^{\alpha_0}$ (@eq-mgf-prod), the
asymptotic intercept attenuation is
$$
\mathrm{plim}\,\hat\beta_{1,\mathrm{Pois}} - \beta_{1,0}
\;=\; \alpha_0\,\log\!\bigl(b_0/(b_0+1)\bigr).
$$ {#eq-pois-attenuation}
At $(\alpha_0, b_0) = (1, 1)$ this gives $\log(1/2) \approx -0.693$;
at $(\alpha_0, b_0) = (2, 1)$ it gives $2\log(1/2) \approx -1.386$.
Both predictions are confirmed by @tbl-sim-bias. The slope $\beta_2$,
by contrast, is approximately unbiased under the Poisson GLM in all cells,
confirming that frontier misspecification contaminates only the intercept
and leaves slope coefficients intact (under the homogeneous DGP, where
$u_i \perp \mathbf{x}_i$).

The Exp frontier is nearly unbiased for all parameters when $\alpha_0 = 1$, with
RMSE declining close to the $\sqrt{n}$ rate expected of a well-posed MLE.
Under Gamma inefficiency ($\alpha_0 = 2$), the misspecified Exp frontier incurs
moderate bias in $\hat{b}$ — which is attempting to fit a one-parameter family
to a two-parameter distribution — while the slope estimates $\hat{\beta}_2$
remain largely unaffected. This suggests a useful robustness property: the
Poisson frontier with exponential inefficiency is a consistent estimator of the
frontier slopes even when the true inefficiency distribution is Gamma.

The correctly specified Gamma frontier recovers all parameters with small
bias and RMSE that declines at the parametric $\sqrt{n}$ rate. At $\alpha_0 = 1$
the bias of $\hat\alpha$ shrinks from $0.13$ at $n = 500$ to near zero
at $n = 2500$, and the Exp-frontier slopes are unaffected by the surplus
shape parameter; at $\alpha_0 = 2$, the Gamma frontier identifies the true
shape with a small downward bias that persists across sample sizes
($-0.11$ at $n = 500$, $-0.08$ at $n = 2500$), reflecting the well-known
incidental-parameters character of $\alpha$ when the frontier mean is
large relative to $b$.

## RMSE Convergence

@fig-sim-rmse plots RMSE against sample size on a log–log scale, with dotted
reference lines of slope $-1/2$ anchored at $n = 500$. Parallelism between the
observed curves and the reference lines confirms that all frontier estimators
achieve the parametric convergence rate.


::: {#cell-fig-sim-rmse .cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code .hidden}
rmse_df <- mc_summary |>
  filter(b_true == 1,
         param  %in% c("beta1", "beta2", "b"),
         model  %in% c("Exp", "Gamma")) |>
  mutate(
    param_label = factor(param,
      levels = c("beta1", "beta2", "b"),
      labels = c(expression(beta[1]), expression(beta[2]), expression(b))),
    alpha_label = factor(alpha_true,
      levels = c(1, 2),
      labels = c(expression(alpha[0]==1), expression(alpha[0]==2))),

  )

ref_df <- rmse_df |>
  filter(n == 500) |>
  select(param_label, alpha_label, Model, rmse_ref = rmse)

rmse_plot_df <- rmse_df |>
  left_join(ref_df, by = c("param_label", "alpha_label", "Model")) |>
  mutate(ref_rmse = rmse_ref * sqrt(500 / n))

ggplot(rmse_df, aes(x = n, y = rmse, colour = Model, shape = Model)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
```

When $\alpha_0 = 1$, the Gamma frontier estimator shows somewhat higher RMSE
than the Exp frontier — particularly for the rate parameter $b$ — reflecting
the cost of estimating the additional shape parameter $\alpha$ from data that
provide limited information about distributional shape. This efficiency loss is
expected: the correctly specified Exp frontier achieves the Cramér–Rao bound for
the restricted model, while the Gamma frontier must estimate one additional
parameter. When $\alpha_0 = 2$, the position reverses: the Gamma frontier's
RMSE for $b$ declines at the parametric rate, while the Exp frontier's $b$ RMSE
does not shrink as quickly due to structural misspecification. Results for
$b \in \{1, 2\}$ are qualitatively similar; the $b = 2$ cells show slightly
faster convergence of $\hat{b}$ due to the tighter inefficiency distribution,
but the coverage and RMSE patterns are unchanged.

![Empirical coverage probability of nominal 95\% confidence intervals for $\beta_1$, $\beta_2$, and $b$ across sample sizes and DGP cells. Dashed line marks the nominal 95\% level. DGP: $b_0 = 1$; $\alpha_0 \in \{1,2\}$ shown in separate columns.](index_files/figure-pdf/fig-sim-coverage-1.pdf){#fig-sim-coverage fig-align='center' fig-pos='H' width=75%}
:::
:::


The correctly specified exponential frontier achieves nominal 95% coverage
for all parameters at $n = 500$ and beyond, consistent with the asymptotic
normality established in @eq-asymp-normal. The Gamma frontier, by contrast,
exhibits chronic delta-method under-coverage in the $\alpha_0 = 1$ panel of
@fig-sim-coverage — typically 70–80% for $\beta_1$ and $b$ — that does not
attenuate with sample size. The cause is a flat $\alpha$-direction in the
log-likelihood: the observed-information Hessian under-states the true
sampling variability of the joint $(\hat\alpha, \hat b)$ pair, so the
delta-method intervals are too short. The slope $\beta_2$ is unaffected
(its Fisher information is decoupled from $\alpha$) and attains nominal
coverage in both specifications. Profile-likelihood intervals for $\alpha$,
illustrated in @fig-profile-lik, sidestep this problem and are the
recommended interval procedure when $\hat\alpha$ is reported.

::: {.remark title="Coverage under misspecification"}
Under $\alpha_0 = 2$, the Exp
frontier's 95% intervals for $b$ may exhibit either over- or under-coverage
depending on the DGP cell, because the parameter being estimated is not the
true $b_0$ but rather the pseudo-true $b^*$ that minimises the Kullback–Leibler
divergence between the exponential model and the Gamma DGP. The correct
interpretation in this case is that the Exp frontier consistently estimates
$b^*$, and the displayed coverage is the coverage of the interval for $b^*$,
not for $b_0$.
:::

Shaded bands show mean $\pm$ 1 SD across replications. DGP: $b_0 = 1$; panels faceted by true $\alpha_0$.](index_files/figure-pdf/fig-sim-mae-1.pdf){#fig-sim-mae fig-align='center' fig-pos='H' width=75%}
:::
:::


MAE declines steadily with $n$ for both estimators across both DGP cells,
as visible in @fig-sim-mae. When $\alpha_0 = 1$, the correctly specified Exp
frontier achieves lower MAE than the Gamma frontier at every sample size, because
the Gamma model must estimate an additional parameter from data that are generated
by the simpler exponential DGP. The gap narrows as $n$ grows, since the Gamma
frontier's estimate of $\alpha$ converges to its true value of 1, recovering the
exponential specification asymptotically.

When $\alpha_0 = 2$, a notable feature emerges: the misspecified Exp frontier
achieves competitive MAE at large $n$, approaching the Gamma frontier's accuracy
despite using the wrong inefficiency distribution. This robustness arises because
efficiency scores depend primarily on the fitted log-frontier $\hat{a}_i =
\mathbf{x}_i'\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ and secondarily on the precise shape of
the conditional density $f(u_i \mid y_i, \mathbf{x}_i)$. The frontier mean is consistently estimated by
both models (as confirmed in @tbl-sim-bias), so the efficiency ranks of units
are largely preserved even under moderate misspecification.

## Summary

The simulation evidence supports three conclusions, all visible in
@tbl-sim-bias, @fig-sim-rmse, @fig-sim-coverage, and @fig-sim-mae. First, the
Poisson frontier MLE is consistent and the bias and RMSE of all parameters
shrink at the parametric $\sqrt{n}$ rate across the grid
$n \in \{500, 1000, 2500\}$. The exponential frontier — when correctly
specified — also attains nominal 95% delta-method coverage at $n = 500$.
The Gamma frontier exhibits delta-method under-coverage of $\hat\alpha$
and $\hat b$ that does not attenuate with sample size; profile-likelihood
intervals are recommended when $\hat\alpha$ is reported. Second, ignoring
the frontier structure (the Poisson GLM) biases the intercept by a
predictable log-mean-inefficiency factor while leaving slope coefficients
intact — a pattern that alerts practitioners to the presence of
inefficiency even when the model is not formally estimated. Third,
efficiency scores are reliably recovered under moderate distributional
misspecification, supporting the practical use of the simpler exponential
frontier as a first-pass diagnostic even in settings where the true
$\alpha$ may differ from 1.

With the finite-sample properties of the estimator established, @sec-empirical
applies the Poisson–Gamma frontier to the @hausman1984 patent panel,
demonstrating that the Gamma shape parameter is empirically relevant and that
the resulting firm-level efficiency scores reveal economically meaningful
variation in innovative productivity.
