:::


### Experimental Design

The experimental design extends the homogeneous framework of @sec-simulations to allow
inefficiency to depend on observed firm characteristics via the scaling model
introduced in @sec-scaling. The data-generating process is

$$
Y_i \mid u_i \;\sim\; \mathrm{Poisson}\!\left(\exp(\mathbf{x}_i'\boldsymbol{\beta}_0 - u_i)\right),
\qquad
u_i \;\sim\; \mathrm{Exp}(b_i),
\qquad
b_i = b_0 \exp\!\left(-\mathbf{z}_i'\boldsymbol{\delta}_0\right),
$$ {#eq-dgp-scaling}

with $\mathbf{x}_i = (1, x_{i1}, x_{i2})'$, $x_{ij} \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim}
\mathrm{Uniform}(0, 5)$, true frontier coefficients $\boldsymbol{\beta}_0 = (1,
0.5, 0.3)'$, and baseline rate $b_0 = 2$. The inefficiency determinant vector is
$\mathbf{z}_i = (z_{i1}, z_{i2})'$, where $z_{i1} \sim \mathrm{Bernoulli}(0.5)$
(a binary characteristic such as ownership type) and $z_{i2} \sim N(0, 1)$ (a
continuous characteristic such as log firm age). This two-component design
represents the type of heterogeneity most commonly encountered in applied work:
a group indicator and a continuous firm-level covariate. The true $b_i$ varies
continuously across firms, so the efficiency score @eq-te-scaling is
observation-specific even after conditioning on $\boldsymbol{\beta}_0$.

The $\boldsymbol{\delta}_0$ vector is varied across four scenarios. Scenario 0
($\boldsymbol{\delta}_0 = \mathbf{0}$) is the null under which no determinants
are present; it is used to verify size control of the likelihood ratio test. Scenario 1
($\boldsymbol{\delta}_0 = (0.3, 0.2)'$) represents weak heterogeneity; Scenario 2
($\boldsymbol{\delta}_0 = (1.0, 0.5)'$) represents strong heterogeneity in which
the most- and least-efficient firms differ substantially in their $b_i$; and
Scenario 3 ($\boldsymbol{\delta}_0 = (0.8, -0.4)'$) introduces a sign reversal
so that one determinant reduces inefficiency while the other increases it. Three
sample sizes $n \in \{500, 1000, 2500\}$ are crossed with the four $\boldsymbol{\delta}_0$
scenarios, yielding twelve experimental cells, each replicated $R = 1{,}000$ times.

Three estimators are evaluated in each replication. The one-step scaling MLE
jointly maximises the log-likelihood @eq-loglik-scaling over $(\boldsymbol{\beta},
b, \boldsymbol{\delta})$ using bound-constrained optimisation with a warm
start from the homogeneous solution. The homogeneous MLE ignores $\mathbf{z}_i$ and
forces $\boldsymbol{\delta} = \mathbf{0}$; it is the correctly specified model in
Scenario 0 and increasingly misspecified in Scenarios 1–3. The two-step estimator
of @wangSchmidt2002 provides a widely used benchmark: the homogeneous model is
fitted first to obtain efficiency scores, and $\boldsymbol{\delta}$ is then
recovered by regressing $-\log\widehat{\mathrm{TE}}_i$ on $\mathbf{z}_i$ by
OLS. By the Cramér–Rao bound the one-step MLE should dominate the two-step
estimator in large samples, but the two-step may be competitive in small samples
where the joint curvature of the scaling likelihood is weak.

In Scenario 0 the LR test @eq-loglik-scaling should achieve size close to the
nominal 5% level — confirming that adding redundant $\boldsymbol{\delta}$
parameters does not spuriously improve the fit — and the frontier coefficients
$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ should be unaffected by the inclusion of unnecessary
determinants. Scenarios 1–3 document the bias incurred by ignoring determinants
and the power of the LR test to detect them.

### Bias and RMSE of Frontier Coefficients


::: {#tbl-scaling-rmse .cell layout-align="center" tbl-cap='Bias and RMSE of frontier coefficient $\beta_1$ (intercept) and rate $b$ across scenarios and sample sizes. Scaling = one-step MLE; Homogeneous = misspecified model. True values: $\beta_1 = 1$, $b_0 = 2$.'}

```{.r .cell-code .hidden}
fmt3 <- function(x) ifelse(is.na(x), "---", sprintf("%.3f", x))

bias_rows <- do.call(rbind, lapply(0:3, function(sid) {
  do.call(rbind, lapply(c(500, 1000, 2500), function(nv) {
    sub <- sc_df[sc_df$scenario == sid & sc_df$n == nv, ]
    conv_sc_ok <- is.finite(sub$b_sc) & is.finite(sub$b1_sc)
    conv_ho_ok <- is.finite(sub$b_ho) & is.finite(sub$b1_ho)

    b1_sc_bias <- mean(sub$b1_sc[conv_sc_ok] - beta_tr[1], na.rm = TRUE)
    b1_sc_rmse <- sqrt(mean((sub$b1_sc[conv_sc_ok] - beta_tr[1])^2, na.rm = TRUE))
    b_sc_bias  <- mean(sub$b_sc[conv_sc_ok]  - b_tr,       na.rm = TRUE)
    b_sc_rmse  <- sqrt(mean((sub$b_sc[conv_sc_ok] - b_tr)^2,  na.rm = TRUE))

    b1_ho_bias <- mean(sub$b1_ho[conv_ho_ok] - beta_tr[1], na.rm = TRUE)
    b1_ho_rmse <- sqrt(mean((sub$b1_ho[conv_ho_ok] - beta_tr[1])^2, na.rm = TRUE))
    b_ho_bias  <- mean(sub$b_ho[conv_ho_ok]  - b_tr,       na.rm = TRUE)
    b_ho_rmse  <- sqrt(mean((sub$b_ho[conv_ho_ok] - b_tr)^2,  na.rm = TRUE))

    data.frame(
      Scenario = scenario_labels[as.character(sid)],

@tbl-scaling-rmse reveals the central finding of this experiment. In Scenario 0
the two estimators perform equivalently — both are approximately unbiased for
$\beta_1$ and $b$, confirming that adding redundant determinant parameters does not
contaminate the frontier estimates. In Scenario 2, the homogeneous model exhibits
substantial upward bias in $\hat{b}$: by forcing $\boldsymbol{\delta} = \mathbf{0}$,
the estimator absorbs the heterogeneous inefficiency into a single inflated
rate parameter and consequently understates the implied mean inefficiency
$1/b$. The scaling MLE remains approximately unbiased for $b$ across
all scenarios and sample sizes, as the parameter $b$ now represents the baseline
rate conditional on $\mathbf{z}_i = \mathbf{0}$ rather than an unconditional
average. The slope coefficient $\hat{\beta}_1$ is largely unaffected by model
misspecification in both estimators — the frontier mean is consistently estimated
even when the inefficiency distribution is mis-specified, echoing the robustness
property observed in @sec-simulations.

@tbl-delta-rmse shows that the scaling MLE recovers both determinants with
small bias and declining RMSE as $n$ grows, consistent with
$\sqrt{n}$-consistency: at $n = 500$ the RMSE of $\hat\delta_1$ is $\approx

replications. Empirical coverage of the nominal 95% delta-method intervals
sits at 94–96% for both determinants across the entire grid
$n \in \{500, 1000, 2500\}$ and all three non-null scenarios — close to
nominal at every cell, in contrast to the chronic under-coverage of the
homogeneous-MC Gamma-frontier intervals discussed above.

### Power of the Likelihood Ratio Test


::: {#cell-fig-lr-power .cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code .hidden}
critical_val <- qchisq(0.95, df = 2)

lr_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(0:3, function(sid) {

The test statistic is $\Lambda = 2(\ell_{\text{scaling}} - \ell_{\text{homogeneous}}) \stackrel{d}{\to} \chi^2_2$ under $H_0$.](index_files/figure-pdf/fig-lr-power-1.pdf){#fig-lr-power fig-align='center' fig-pos='H' width=75%}

@fig-lr-power confirms correct size control in Scenario 0: the rejection rate is
close to 5% across all sample sizes, validating the $\chi^2_2$ reference
distribution. In Scenario 2 the test achieves near-complete power by $n = 500$,
reflecting the large signal-to-noise ratio when $\boldsymbol{\delta}_0 = (1.0,
0.5)'$. Scenario 3, with a sign reversal, has slightly lower power at $n = 500$ than
Scenario 2, because the binary and continuous determinants partially offset
each other in terms of likelihood curvature; by $n = 1000$ the power gap
disappears. Weak determinants (Scenario 1) require $n \approx 2500$ for
rejection rates near 80%, suggesting that the test has limited power
against subtle heterogeneity with fewer than $\sim 1000$ observations.

@fig-score-mae documents the distortion in efficiency scores caused by ignoring
inefficiency determinants. In Scenario 0, both models produce virtually identical
MAE because the DGP is homogeneous. In Scenario 2, the homogeneous model assigns
the same expected inefficiency to every firm regardless of $\mathbf{z}_i$,
systematically overestimating efficiency for high-$b_i$ firms (those with low
inefficiency) and underestimating it for low-$b_i$ firms. The scaling model, by
contrast, adapts its conditional density to each unit's $\hat{b}_i$, achieving substantially
lower MAE across all sample sizes. The cost of ignoring determinants is
proportional to the strength of $\boldsymbol{\delta}_0$: Scenario 1 shows a small
gap, Scenario 2 a large one, and Scenario 3 an intermediate gap consistent with
the opposing directions of the two components.

The scaling MLE converged in
98.7% of all replications across the twelve
experimental cells, with a derivative-free fallback invoked in less than 5%
of runs. In a small fraction of replications
(0.00% across all cells) the
series-based efficiency score evaluator returns NA due to catastrophic
cancellation when the positive and negative partial sums are nearly equal; these
replications are excluded from the MAE computation reported in @fig-score-mae.
Wall-clock time per (scenario, $n$) cell, on 8 parallel cores, ranged from
53 to
321 seconds. All computations rely on
standard incomplete-gamma and log-gamma routines; no specialised count-data
or frontier packages are required.

### Summary

The scaling model simulation establishes four conclusions. First, the one-step
MLE accurately recovers both frontier coefficients and inefficiency determinants
with coverage approaching 95% by $n = 500$, and the LR test achieves correct
size in Scenario 0 and rejects with high frequency against moderate-to-strong alternatives by
$n = 500$. Second, ignoring determinants — fitting the homogeneous model to
heterogeneous data — produces upward bias in $\hat{b}$ that is proportional to
the strength of $\boldsymbol{\delta}_0$; this bias directly distorts the implied
mean inefficiency and the efficiency rankings across firms. Third, the two-step
estimator provides a useful heuristic for preliminary analysis but is dominated
by the one-step MLE in terms of RMSE for $\boldsymbol{\delta}$ at all but the
smallest sample sizes, consistent with the theoretical efficiency advantage of
full MLE. Fourth, efficiency score accuracy degrades substantially in Scenario 2
under the homogeneous model but is largely unaffected under the scaling model,
supporting the recommendation of @sec-scaling to include firm-level determinants
whenever $\mathbf{z}_i$ is available and there is prior reason to expect
heterogeneous inefficiency levels.

```
:::


The production-frontier results in @sec-simulations rest on the closed-form
alternating series @eq-pmf-gamma. The cost frontier carries no analogous
series (@prp-pmf, part ii); each per-observation log-PMF is evaluated by
adaptive quadrature on the $v = e^{a_i + u}$ substitution described in
@sec-estimation. This subsection validates the cost-orientation MLE on the
exponential corollary $\alpha = 1$, where the closed form @eq-cost-pmf
applies whenever the upper-incomplete-gamma shape $y_i - b$ is positive
and the quadrature path handles the negative-shape regime transparently.

### Experimental Design

The cost-frontier DGP mirrors @eq-dgp with the sign of the inefficiency
reversed:

$$
Y_i \mid u_i \;\sim\; \mathrm{Poisson}\!\left(\exp(\mathbf{x}_i'\boldsymbol{\beta}_0 + u_i)\right),
\qquad u_i \;\sim\; \mathrm{Exp}(b_0),
$$ {#eq-dgp-cost}

with $\mathbf{x}_i = (1, z_i)'$, $z_i \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} N(0, 1)$,
and $\boldsymbol{\beta}_0 = (1,\, 0.5)'$. The rate parameter is varied as
$b_0 \in \{2, 3\}$; both values satisfy $b_0 > 1$, the existence threshold
for the marginal mean $\mathrm{E}[Y_i] = \lambda_i\,b_0/(b_0 - 1)$
established in @sec-existence. Three sample sizes
$n \in \{500, 1000, 2500\}$ are crossed with the two $b_0$ values, yielding
six experimental cells and $R = 100$ replications each. The
smaller $R$ reflects the higher per-fit compute cost of the cost
orientation: each likelihood evaluation invokes one `stats::integrate`
call per observation rather than an $O(K)$ alternating-series sum.

Two estimators are evaluated in each replication. The *Poisson GLM* is the
same misspecified benchmark used in @sec-simulations; here it consistently
estimates $\log\mathrm{E}[Y_i \mid \mathbf{x}_i] = a_i + \alpha \log\bigl(b_0/(b_0-1)\bigr)$,
so the slope coefficient $\beta_2$ is again asymptotically unbiased while
the intercept is biased *upward* by the predicted log-mean-inefficiency
factor (the sign reversal relative to the production case discussed in
@sec-estimation). The *Exp-cost frontier* fits the cost-orientation
likelihood with $\alpha = 1$ fixed; it is correctly specified across all
cells.

### Parameter Recovery


::: {#tbl-cost-bias .cell layout-align="center" tbl-cap='Bias, RMSE, and 95\% delta-method coverage of the Poisson GLM and the exponential cost frontier across sample sizes and DGP cells. True values: $\beta_1 = 1$, $\beta_2 = 0.5$, $b = b_0$. Sample sizes: $n \in \{500, 1000, 2500\}$. Replications per cell: $R = 100$. The Poisson GLM has no $b$ parameter (shown as ---).'}

```{.r .cell-code .hidden}
fmt3 <- function(x) ifelse(is.na(x), "---", sprintf("%.3f", x))

build_cost_row <- function(b0, n_val, param) {
  get_row <- function(mod) {
    r <- cost_summ[cost_summ$b_true == b0 &
                     cost_summ$n     == n_val &
                     cost_summ$model == mod &
                     cost_summ$param == param, ]
    if (nrow(r) == 0) return(c("---", "---", "---"))
    c(fmt3(r$bias), fmt3(r$rmse), fmt3(r$coverage))
  }
  pois <- get_row("Poisson")
  ec   <- get_row("Exp-cost")
  data.frame(
    `$n$`         = n_val,
    Parameter     = switch(param,
                       beta1 = "$\\beta_1$",
                       beta2 = "$\\beta_2$",
                       b     = "$b$"),
    `Bias (Pois)` = pois[1], `RMSE (Pois)` = pois[2], `Cov.\\ (Pois)` = pois[3],
    `Bias (EC)`   = ec[1],   `RMSE (EC)`   = ec[2],   `Cov.\\ (EC)`   = ec[3],
    check.names = FALSE, stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
}

  )
  panels_c[[length(panels_c) + 1]] <- do.call(rbind, rows)
}

get_pois_b1_bias <- function(b0, n_val) {
  r <- cost_summ[cost_summ$b_true == b0 & cost_summ$n == n_val &
                   cost_summ$model == "Poisson" & cost_summ$param == "beta1", ]
  r$bias
}
predicted_b1_inflation_b2 <- log(2 / 1)   # alpha log(b/(b-1)) at alpha=1, b=2
predicted_b1_inflation_b3 <- log(3 / 2)   # at b = 3
observed_b1_inflation_b2  <- get_pois_b1_bias(2, 2500)
observed_b1_inflation_b3  <- get_pois_b1_bias(3, 2500)

ec_b1_rmse_500  <- cost_summ$rmse[cost_summ$b_true == 2 & cost_summ$n == 500  &
                                    cost_summ$model == "Exp-cost" & cost_summ$param == "beta1"]
ec_b1_rmse_2500 <- cost_summ$rmse[cost_summ$b_true == 2 & cost_summ$n == 2500 &
                                    cost_summ$model == "Exp-cost" & cost_summ$param == "beta1"]
sqrt5_ratio <- sqrt(2500 / 500)
```
:::


@tbl-cost-bias confirms two predictions from the theory. First, the
Poisson GLM intercept is biased upward, the magnitude matching the formula
$\alpha_0 \log\bigl(b_0/(b_0 - 1)\bigr)$ derived in @sec-estimation: at
$(\alpha_0, b_0) = (1, 2)$ the prediction is $\log 2 \approx
0.693$ and the observed bias at
$n = 2500$ is $0.693$; at
$b_0 = 3$ the prediction is $\log(3/2) \approx
0.405$ and the observed bias is
$0.406$. The slope $\beta_2$ is
nearly unbiased under the Poisson GLM in every cell, replicating the
production-case finding that frontier misspecification contaminates only
the intercept under a homogeneous DGP.

Second, the cost-frontier MLE recovers all three parameters at the
parametric $\sqrt{n}$ rate. The RMSE of $\hat\beta_1$ at $b_0 = 2$ falls
from $0.049$ at $n = 500$ to
$0.020$ at $n = 2500$, a ratio of
$2.45$ against the
$\sqrt{2500/500} = 2.24$ benchmark. The
analogous ratio for $\hat b$ is similar. Delta-method coverage sits at
roughly 92–97% across cells, close to the nominal 95%; the small
fluctuations reflect the smaller $R = 100$ relative to the
$R = 1000$ used in @sec-simulations.

### Efficiency-Score Accuracy


::: {.cell .hidden layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code .hidden}
mae_b2_500  <- cost_summ$bias[cost_summ$b_true == 2 & cost_summ$n == 500  &
                                cost_summ$model == "Exp-cost" & cost_summ$param == "TE_MAE"]
mae_b2_2500 <- cost_summ$bias[cost_summ$b_true == 2 & cost_summ$n == 2500 &
                                cost_summ$model == "Exp-cost" & cost_summ$param == "TE_MAE"]
mae_b3_500  <- cost_summ$bias[cost_summ$b_true == 3 & cost_summ$n == 500  &
                                cost_summ$model == "Exp-cost" & cost_summ$param == "TE_MAE"]
mae_b3_2500 <- cost_summ$bias[cost_summ$b_true == 3 & cost_summ$n == 2500 &
                                cost_summ$model == "Exp-cost" & cost_summ$param == "TE_MAE"]
```
:::


The efficiency-score MAE — the average absolute deviation between
$\widehat{\mathrm{TE}}_i$ and the realised oracle score $e^{-u_i}$ —
declines only mildly with $n$, from
$0.130$ at $(b_0 = 2, n = 500)$ to
$0.129$ at $n = 2500$, and from
$0.124$ to $0.124$
in the $b_0 = 3$ panel. This irreducible noise floor reflects the
posterior conditional variance of $e^{-u_i}$ given $y_i$ in @eq-post-cost,
which does *not* shrink with $n$ — only the parameter estimates feeding
into the conditional density do. The closed-form upper-incomplete-gamma
score (@eq-te-formula-cost) is used for observations with $y_i > b + 1$;
the remaining negative-shape observations are handled by quadrature on
the $v$-substitution discussed in @sec-estimation, with no excess MAE
attributable to the regime switch.

### Summary

The cost-frontier MLE delivers the same parametric $\sqrt{n}$ convergence
and near-nominal coverage as the production-case estimator, despite
relying on per-observation quadrature rather than an alternating-series
sum. The Poisson GLM diagnostic carries over with its sign reversed —
upward intercept bias of magnitude $\alpha_0 \log\bigl(b_0/(b_0-1)\bigr)$
— providing practitioners with an orientation-aware indicator that a
one-sided Gamma frontier is needed even before the cost-frontier MLE is
fitted. The efficiency-score MAE saturates at the conditional-variance
noise floor, as in the production case. Together with the existence
analysis of @sec-existence, these results establish that both
orientations of the Poisson--Gamma frontier are operational at every
$b > 0$, with the cost-orientation marginal-mean restriction $b > 1$
binding only on reporting summary statistics, not on estimation or
efficiency scoring.

# fit. The multi-start may report convergence = 0 while sitting on the

gam_converged <- !is.null(fit_gam) &&
                 isTRUE(fit_gam$convergence == 0L) &&

                 fit_gam$loglik > -1e10 &&

## Data {#sec-emp-data}

We apply the Poisson stochastic frontier to the patent panel introduced by
@hausman1984, which has served as the canonical benchmark for count-data
econometrics for four decades [@cameronTrivedi2005, Ch. 23;
@hallGrilichesHausman1986]. The sample comprises
346 U.S. manufacturing firms observed
annually over five years (1968–1972), yielding 1730 pooled firm-year
observations. We treat the data as a pooled cross-section and include year
fixed effects to absorb aggregate time trends, following the approach of
@feHofler2013 for count-data frontiers.

The outcome is the annual patent count $Y_{it}$. As @tbl-data-summary
shows, the distribution is markedly right-skewed, ranging from
0 to 515 with a mean of 34.77 and standard
deviation of 70.88. Zero-patent firm-years account for
19.5% of the sample, confirming the degree of
zero-inflation documented by @hallGrilichesHausman1986. The variance-to-mean
ratio of 144.5 far exceeds 1, indicating severe
overdispersion relative to a Poisson model — a pattern the frontier structure
attributes to heterogeneous technical inefficiency rather than pure
unobserved heterogeneity.

The frontier log-mean is parameterised as

$$
a_{it} \;=\; \mathbf{x}_{it}'\boldsymbol{\beta}
\;=\; \beta_1 + \beta_2 \log R_{it} + \beta_3 \log K_{it}
     + \beta_4 \cdot \mathbf{1}[\text{Science}]_i + \sum_{t=2}^{5}\delta_t d_t,
$$ {#eq-frontier-spec}

where $\log R_{it}$ is log R&D expenditure (the primary innovation input),
$\log K_{it}$ is log capital stock, $\mathbf{1}[\text{Science}]_i$ is a
time-invariant science-sector indicator, and $d_t$ are year dummies.^[The
year indicator coefficients in @eq-frontier-spec are components of the frontier
coefficient vector $\boldsymbol{\beta}$; the symbol $\delta$ is reserved
exclusively for the scaling parameters in @eq-scaling-bi.]


::: {#tbl-data-summary .cell layout-align="center" tbl-cap='Summary statistics for the Hausman--Hall--Griliches patent panel (1730 firm-year observations, 346 firms, 1968--1972). The variance-to-mean ratio of the patent count far exceeds 1, indicating overdispersion that the standard Poisson regression cannot accommodate.'}

## MLE Results {#sec-emp-results}

@tbl-results-main reports maximum likelihood estimates for two specifications:
the standard Poisson GLM (misspecified benchmark) and the Poisson frontier
with exponential inefficiency ($\alpha = 1$ fixed). The Gamma frontier was
attempted but did not achieve numerical convergence on this dataset, a finding
discussed in @sec-conclusion. All models are
estimated on the pooled sample; standard errors are in parentheses.


::: {#tbl-results-main .cell layout-align="center" tbl-cap='MLE estimates for the homogeneous specifications on the patent panel: standard Poisson GLM (misspecified benchmark) and Poisson frontier with exponential inefficiency. Standard errors (delta method for $\hat{b}$) in parentheses. Year fixed effects included but not reported. Log-lik.\ and AIC computed at the maximum. The Gamma frontier with free $\alpha$ did not converge on the pooled cross-section; convergence behaviour is discussed in @sec-emp-scaling.'}

```{.r .cell-code .hidden}
make_coef_row <- function(fit_obj, is_glm = FALSE) {
  fmt <- function(v, s) sprintf("%.3f (%.3f)", v, s)
  if (is_glm) {
    cf <- coef(fit_obj)
    se <- sqrt(diag(vcov(fit_obj)))
    list(
      Intercept = fmt(cf[1],       se[1]),
      log_RD    = fmt(cf["rd"],    se["rd"]),
      log_K     = fmt(cf["lk"],    se["lk"]),
      sci_sect  = fmt(cf["sci"],   se["sci"]),
      b         = "---",
      alpha     = "---",
      LogLik    = round(as.numeric(logLik(fit_obj)), 1),
      AIC       = round(AIC(fit_obj), 1)
    )
  } else {
    cf <- fit_obj$coefficients
    se <- fit_obj$se
    list(
      Intercept = fmt(cf[1], se[1]),
      log_RD    = fmt(cf[2], se[2]),
      log_K     = fmt(cf[3], se[3]),
      sci_sect  = fmt(cf[4], se[4]),
      b         = fmt(fit_obj$b, fit_obj$se_b),
      alpha     = if (!is.na(fit_obj$alpha) && fit_obj$dist == "gamma")
                    fmt(fit_obj$alpha, fit_obj$se_alpha)
                  else "1 (fixed)",
      LogLik    = round(fit_obj$loglik, 1),
      AIC       = round(fit_obj$AIC,   1)
    )
  }
}

r_gam  <- if (gam_converged) make_coef_row(fit_gam) else NULL

The pattern in @tbl-results-main aligns with the theoretical predictions from
@sec-simulations. The Poisson GLM intercept (0.732) is attenuated
relative to the frontier specification (1.911), reflecting the
log-mean-inefficiency bias identified in the Monte Carlo study. The slope on
log R&D is positive and significant across both specifications, replicating
the long-established empirical relationship between research spending and
patent counts [@pakesGriliches1980; @hausman1984].^[As an external check, refitting the pooled Poisson GLM with the current value and four lags of $\log R$ recovers a sum-of-elasticities of $0.486$, which exactly matches the corresponding entry in @cameronTrivedi2005, Table 23.1. Adding the same four lags to the frontier specifications, however, induces severe multicollinearity along the $b$-direction and prevents both the exponential and Gamma likelihoods from converging to a finite interior maximum.] The capital stock coefficient is positive but smaller in
magnitude than the R&D coefficient, consistent with the labour-intensive nature
of knowledge production. Science-sector firms have a systematically higher
frontier mean, reflecting the superior technological opportunities available in
chemistry, electronics, and scientific instruments.

The estimated rate $\hat{b}$ from the exponential frontier implies an average
inefficiency of $\mathrm{E}[u_i] = 1/\hat{b}$ in log-output units: the
average firm's expected output is $\exp(-1/\hat{b})$ times its frontier
output, with the full distribution of inefficiency heterogeneity described
by the fitted exponential density.

::: {.remark title="Panel structure and standard errors."}
The patent dataset is a balanced panel of 346 firms observed over five years. The standard errors reported in
@tbl-results-main are based on the inverse Hessian and do not account for within-firm serial correlation. Cluster-robust standard errors (clustered at
the firm level) would be more conservative; their computation is deferred to
future work. Coefficient estimates are unaffected by this choice.
:::

::: {.remark title="Gamma frontier convergence"}
The Gamma frontier with free $\alpha$ did not converge on the patent panel.
The likelihood surface is flat along the ridge $\alpha \to 0$, $b \to \infty$
at constant $\alpha/b$ (see @sec-estimation), and with $n = 1730$ pooled
observations the curvature in the $\alpha$ direction is insufficient for
the optimiser to locate an interior maximum reliably. We therefore retain
the exponential frontier as the preferred homogeneous specification here. A
related diagnostic from the scaling-model fit of @sec-emp-scaling — where the
free-$\alpha$ Gamma frontier does converge but to $\hat\alpha = 1.000$ at the
exponential boundary — is reported alongside the LR test in that section.
:::

## Efficiency Scores {#sec-emp-efficiency}

The technical efficiency of firm $i$ in year $t$ is the conditional
expectation

$$
\widehat{\mathrm{TE}}_{it}
\;=\; \mathrm{E}\!\left[e^{-u_{it}} \mid Y_{it} = y_{it},\, \mathbf{x}_{it}\right],
$$ {#eq-te-emp}

computed from the preferred (exponential) specification via the
formula developed in @sec-model. The mean estimated efficiency
across the sample is 0.291, indicating
that the average firm achieves roughly
29.1% of its frontier patent
output, as shown in @fig-eff-hist. The distribution is left-skewed, with most
firms bunched near the frontier and a tail of chronically inefficient units.

       y = expression(widehat(TE)[it])) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position="bottom")

@fig-eff-scatter reveals a positive cross-sectional association between R&D
intensity and estimated efficiency: firms that invest more in research tend to
operate closer to the patent frontier, controlling for capital stock and sector.
Science-sector firms cluster in the upper-right quadrant, consistent with the
view that technological opportunity amplifies the returns to R&D investment in
high-opportunity industries. The association is approximately log-linear in both
sectors, with no evidence of diminishing returns to R&D intensity within the
observed range.

@fig-eff-year documents the time profile of efficiency over the 1968–1972
period. The median efficiency is broadly stable across years, suggesting that
aggregate technological change is adequately absorbed by the year fixed effects
in the frontier specification @eq-frontier-spec. Any residual upward drift
in the distribution over time is consistent with learning-by-doing or the gradual
adoption of more productive research practices over the period.

::: {.remark title="Panel structure and unit effects"}
The present application treats
the data as a pooled cross-section, so the efficiency score $\widehat{\mathrm{TE}}_{it}$
varies both across firms and across years within a firm. A natural extension would
be to introduce firm fixed effects into the frontier mean $a_{it}$, which would
partial out time-invariant heterogeneity and allow $u_{it}$ to capture only
within-firm year-to-year inefficiency. Such a panel frontier is left for future
work (see @sec-conclusion for discussion), but the stability visible in
@fig-eff-year suggests that the pooled estimates are not severely contaminated
by time-invariant unobserved heterogeneity over this short five-year window.
:::

The empirical results confirm that the exponential frontier adds meaningful
structure over the standard Poisson GLM: the frontier intercept shift is
consistent with the Monte Carlo predictions, the slope estimates are stable
across both specifications, and the resulting efficiency scores reveal
economically interpretable cross-firm and cross-sector variation. The
scaling model extension in @sec-emp-scaling examines how firm-level capital
intensity and sector membership shape the distribution of inefficiency.
@sec-conclusion collects the paper's contributions and outlines extensions
to panel data, zero-inflated counts, and Bayesian estimation.

## Inefficiency Determinants {#sec-emp-scaling}

The homogeneous frontier estimated in @sec-emp-results constrains every firm
to draw from the same inefficiency distribution, implying a common mean
$\mathrm{E}(u_i) = 1/b$. Systematic firm-level characteristics — in particular
capital intensity and sector membership — may shift the level of inefficiency
independently of the frontier mean $a_i$. This section extends the analysis
using the scaling model of @sec-scaling, which writes
$b_i = b\exp(-z_i'\delta)$ so that $\mathrm{E}(u_i) = \exp(z_i'\delta)/b$
is firm-specific [@caudillFord1993; @wangSchmidt2002].

Two inefficiency determinants are selected on substantive economic grounds.
The first is log capital stock ($z_1 = \log K_i$). Capital-intensive firms
may be better positioned to translate R&D investment into patents through
complementarity between physical and knowledge capital: laboratory equipment,
manufacturing prototypes, and testing infrastructure all lower the per-patent
cost of the innovation process. This implies $\delta_1 < 0$, so that more
capital reduces inefficiency by raising $b_i$. The second determinant is the
science-sector indicator ($z_2 = \mathbf{1}[\text{Science}]_i$). Firms in
chemistry, electronics, and scientific instruments maintain dedicated IP
practices, routinised patenting workflows, and deeper absorptive capacity
[@hallGrilichesHausman1986], all of which reduce the gap between actual
output and the patent frontier. The sign prediction is again $\delta_2 < 0$.
Log R&D expenditure is deliberately excluded from $z_i$: it already enters
the frontier mean $a_i$ through $\mathbf{x}_i$, so including it in $z_i$
would conflate $\beta_{\log R}$ and $\delta$. An optional quadratic term
$z_3 = (\log K_i)^2$ is retained in the extended model (M5) only if the
likelihood ratio test against M4 rejects at the 5\% level.


::: {.cell .hidden layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code .hidden}
stopifnot(file.exists("results/empirical_fits.rds"),
          file.exists("results/efficiency_scores.rds"))

ef  <- readRDS("results/empirical_fits.rds")
sc  <- readRDS("results/efficiency_scores.rds")

dat_sc <- ef$data
y_sc   <- ef$y
X_sc   <- ef$X
p_sc   <- ncol(X_sc)

fit_pois_sc <- ef$fit_pois
fit_M2_sc   <- ef$fit_M2
fit_M3_sc   <- ef$fit_exp
fit_M4_sc   <- ef$fit_M4
fit_M5_sc   <- ef$fit_M5
fit_M6_sc   <- ef$fit_M6

ll_M1_sc <- ef$ll_M1
ll_M2_sc <- ef$ll_M2
ll_M3_sc <- ef$ll_M3
ll_M4_sc <- ef$ll_M4
ll_M5_sc <- ef$ll_M5

se_M4_sc    <- ef$se_M4
se_M5_sc    <- ef$se_M5
lr_tests_sc <- ef$lr_tests
pref_sc     <- ef$preferred_model

# Preferred scaling fit
fit_pref_sc <- if (pref_sc == "M5" && !is.null(fit_M5_sc)) fit_M5_sc else fit_M4_sc
ll_pref_sc  <- if (pref_sc == "M5") ll_M5_sc  else ll_M4_sc
se_pref_sc  <- if (pref_sc == "M5") se_M5_sc  else se_M4_sc
Z_pref_sc   <- if (pref_sc == "M5") ef$Z_full else ef$Z_base
q_pref_sc   <- ncol(Z_pref_sc)

m6_converged_sc <- !is.null(fit_M6_sc)

# Column positions in X_sc (always: 1=Intercept, 2=rd, 3=lk, 4=sci, 5+=year FEs)
# Using fixed positions because X colnames differ across GLM and frontier objects
idx_int <- 1L
idx_rd  <- 2L
idx_lk  <- 3L
idx_sci <- 4L

# Helper: format estimate (SE) with significance stars
fmt_s <- function(est, se) {
  if (is.null(est) || is.na(est) || is.null(se) || is.na(se)) return("---")
  z  <- abs(est / se)
  pv <- 2 * pnorm(-z)
  s  <- if (pv < 0.01) "***" else if (pv < 0.05) "**" else if (pv < 0.10) "*" else ""
  paste0(sprintf("%.3f", est), " (", sprintf("%.3f", se), ")", s)
}

# GLM
cf_glm <- coef(fit_pois_sc)
se_glm <- sqrt(diag(vcov(fit_pois_sc)))

# M2 (PHN) SEs from Hessian
if (!is.null(fit_M2_sc) && !is.null(fit_M2_sc$hessian)) {
  se_M2_all <- tryCatch(
    sqrt(pmax(diag(solve(fit_M2_sc$hessian)), 0)),
    error = function(e) rep(NA_real_, length(fit_M2_sc$par))
  )
} else {
  se_M2_all <- rep(NA_real_, p_sc + 1L)
}

# M3 (Exp homog): fit_poisson_frontier S3 object
cf_M3 <- if (!is.null(fit_M3_sc)) fit_M3_sc$coefficients else rep(NA_real_, p_sc)
se_M3 <- if (!is.null(fit_M3_sc)) fit_M3_sc$se           else rep(NA_real_, p_sc)
b_M3  <- if (!is.null(fit_M3_sc)) fit_M3_sc$b            else NA_real_
sb_M3 <- if (!is.null(fit_M3_sc)) fit_M3_sc$se_b         else NA_real_

# Preferred scaling: b and delta
b_pref_sc     <- exp(fit_pref_sc$par[p_sc + 1L])
sb_pref_sc    <- se_pref_sc[p_sc + 1L] * b_pref_sc   # delta method
delta_pref    <- fit_pref_sc$par[(p_sc + 2L):(p_sc + 1L + q_pref_sc)]
se_delta_pref <- se_pref_sc[(p_sc + 2L):(p_sc + 1L + q_pref_sc)]

# AIC
n_obs  <- length(y_sc)
aic_M1 <- AIC(fit_pois_sc)
aic_M2 <- if (!is.null(fit_M2_sc) && is.finite(ll_M2_sc))
  2 * (p_sc + 1) - 2 * ll_M2_sc else NA_real_
aic_M3 <- if (!is.null(fit_M3_sc)) fit_M3_sc$AIC else NA_real_
aic_M4 <- if (!is.null(fit_M4_sc) && is.finite(ll_M4_sc))
  2 * (p_sc + 1 + 2) - 2 * ll_M4_sc else NA_real_
aic_M5 <- if (!is.null(fit_M5_sc) && is.finite(ll_M5_sc))
  2 * (p_sc + 1 + 3) - 2 * ll_M5_sc else NA_real_
aic_pref <- if (pref_sc == "M5") aic_M5 else aic_M4

# Mean efficiency
te_mean_M3   <- round(mean(sc$te_homog,   na.rm = TRUE), 3)
te_mean_pref <- round(mean(sc$te_scaling, na.rm = TRUE), 3)

# Delta summary for prose
delta1_hat  <- delta_pref[1L]
delta2_hat  <- delta_pref[2L]
delta1_se   <- se_delta_pref[1L]
delta2_se   <- se_delta_pref[2L]

mean_eu    <- mean(1 / sc$b_i_pref, na.rm = TRUE)   # mean E[u_i]
me_logk    <- delta1_hat * mean_eu                    # marginal effect at mean z
ineff_ratio <- exp(delta2_hat)                        # sci vs non-sci inefficiency ratio

# Model-comparison test objects
ic31 <- lr_tests_sc$IC_31   # AIC/BIC gap: M3 vs M1 (boundary; no formal test)
v32  <- lr_tests_sc$V_32    # Vuong: M3 vs M2 (genuinely non-nested)
lr2  <- lr_tests_sc$LR_2    # Nested LR: M4 vs M3
lr3  <- lr_tests_sc$LR_3    # Nested LR: M5 vs M4
lr4  <- lr_tests_sc$LR_4    # Nested LR: M6 vs M3 (Gamma vs Exp; only present if M6 converged)
```
:::


@tbl-model-comparison collects maximum likelihood estimates for all fitted
specifications. Moving from the Poisson GLM (M1) to the homogeneous
exponential frontier (M3) raises the log-likelihood by
\ensuremath{1.20831\times 10^{4}} log-units at the cost of one additional
parameter, confirming that a one-sided inefficiency term is warranted by
the data. The Poisson half-normal benchmark (M2) of @feHofler2013 achieves a
similar fit to M3. Adding inefficiency determinants in the scaling
specification further raises the log-likelihood while shifting the intercept
and the baseline rate $b$, since firm-specific mean inefficiency is now
partitioned between the homogeneous component and $\exp(-z_i'\delta)$.

@tbl-model-tests reports the model-comparison statistics. We use a
test only where the asymptotic theory it relies on is valid for the
specific comparison.

  - The chi-squared **likelihood-ratio test** is reported for LR$_2$
    (M4 vs M3) and LR$_3$ (M5 vs M4). These are nested within the
    exponential scaling family and the restricted parameter values
    ($\delta = 0$ and $\delta_3 = 0$ respectively) lie in the interior
    of the alternative space, so the standard $\chi^2$ reference
    distribution holds.
  - The **Vuong [-@vuong1989] test** is reported for V$_{32}$
    (M3 vs M2): the exponential Gamma frontier and the Poisson
    half-normal benchmark of @feHofler2013 are distinct distributional
    families, both fitted at parameter values well inside their
    respective interiors, so the standard normal limit
    $V = \sqrt{n}\,\bar m / s_m \to_d \mathcal{N}(0,1)$ under the null
    of equal closeness applies. Sign convention: $V > 0$ favours the
    first-named model.
  - For M3 vs M1 (the exponential frontier versus the plain Poisson
    GLM) we report only the **AIC and BIC gaps**. The Poisson GLM is
    the $b \to \infty$ boundary of the exponential frontier, so the
    two families overlap at a boundary point; under such overlap
    Vuong's normal limit fails [@andrews2001], and the chi-squared LR
    is similarly invalid. The information-criterion gaps are
    boundary-agnostic and characterise the comparison cleanly.


::: {#tbl-model-tests .cell layout-align="center" tbl-cap='Model-comparison statistics. V$_{32}$ is the Vuong (1989) $z$-statistic with two-sided $p$-value from the standard normal; LR$_2$, LR$_3$ (and LR$_4$ when M6 converges) are likelihood-ratio statistics with $\chi^2_{df}$ reference distributions. For M3 vs M1 the boundary nesting precludes standard inference, so we report the AIC and BIC gaps (positive favouring M3) without a formal test. Critical values: standard normal $|z| > 1.96$ at the 5\% level; $\chi^2_1(0.95) = 3.84$; $\chi^2_2(0.95) = 5.99$.'}

The Gamma-shape diagnostic LR$_4$ in @tbl-model-tests bears directly on the
choice between exponential and Gamma inefficiency. With the scaling
structure in place, the free-$\alpha$ Gamma fit (M6) converges to
$\hat\alpha = 1.000$ at the exponential boundary; the test statistic is
LR$_4 = 0.00$ ($p = 1.00$), so the data provide no evidence against the
exponential restriction. This is consistent with the weak-identification
phenomenon documented in @sec-simulations: when the frontier mean
$\lambda_i$ is large relative to $b$ — as it is here, with mean patent
count $\approx 35$ and $\hat b \approx 0.52$ — the curvature of the
log-likelihood in the $\alpha$ direction is shallow, and the sample carries
little information about the shape of the inefficiency density. The
exponential scaling frontier (M4/M5) is therefore retained as the preferred
specification.

The signs and magnitudes of the $\hat{\delta}$ estimates accord with prior
expectations. The capital coefficient
$\hat{\delta}_1 = -0.121$
(SE $= 0.015$)
is negative, consistent with the association between capital intensity and lower estimated inefficiency;
Evaluated at the sample-mean inefficiency level, a one-unit increase in
$\log K_i$ shifts the predicted mean inefficiency by
$\hat{\delta}_1 \times \overline{\mathrm{E}(u_i)} = -0.267$
log-units, a marginal effect derived from
$\partial\,\mathrm{E}(u_i)/\partial z_{i1} = \delta_1 \cdot \mathrm{E}(u_i)$
(see @eq-scaling-bi). The science-sector coefficient
$\hat{\delta}_2 = -0.528$
(SE $= 0.061$) implies that firms in science-intensive
sectors show mean inefficiency 41%
lower than comparable non-science
firms ($\exp(\hat{\delta}_2) = 0.59$), holding log
capital constant. This association is consistent with the superior absorptive
capacity and established IP workflows that characterise chemistry, electronics,
and scientific-instrument firms [@hallGrilichesHausman1986].

@fig-eff-logk-scaling and @fig-eff-sector-scaling illuminate the two channels
through which the determinants shape the efficiency distribution. The positive
slope of the LOESS curves in @fig-eff-logk-scaling shows that capital
stock is negatively associated with estimated inefficiency (positively
associated with efficiency scores), consistent with $\hat{\delta}_1 < 0$.
Firms in science-intensive sectors (blue) show higher efficiency scores
than non-science firms (grey) at comparable capital levels, corroborating
the negative $\hat{\delta}_2$. @fig-eff-sector-scaling makes the
sector gap explicit: the median efficiency of science-sector firms exceeds that
of other firms by approximately
0.162
points, a practically meaningful difference relative to the sample mean of
0.275.

Ignoring these determinants and fitting the homogeneous model (M3) absorbs the
sector- and capital-induced heterogeneity into the single rate parameter $b$,
compressing the efficiency distribution. The firms for which the gap between
scaling and homogeneous scores is largest are precisely the capital-intensive
science-sector observations that the determinants identify as nearest to the
frontier: the homogeneous model systematically under-rates their efficiency,
masking the complementarity between physical capital and innovative capacity.
This pattern mirrors the simulation evidence of @sec-sim-scaling, where
ignoring strong inefficiency determinants was shown to bias the estimated
overall inefficiency level upward. The results support the recommendation of
[@wangSchmidt2002] that one-step estimation with firm-level covariates is
preferable to the homogeneous frontier whenever economic theory motivates a
heterogeneous inefficiency distribution.


# Conclusion {#sec-conclusion}

This paper has derived a closed-form likelihood for the Poisson stochastic
frontier model with Gamma inefficiency. Integrating the conditional Poisson
against the Gamma yields a PMF expressible as an absolutely convergent
alternating series whose $\alpha = 1$ case reduces to the lower incomplete
gamma function (@eq-pmf-exp), and whose general-$\alpha$ form
(@eq-pmf-gamma) is accurate up to truncation of an infinite series.
The result delivers exact maximum likelihood as a tractable alternative to
the simulated likelihood of @feHofler2013, with the same closed-form
treatment available in the cost-frontier orientation and the conditional
technical efficiency score recovered as a ratio of two series. The scaling
extension of @sec-scaling lets observed determinants enter the inefficiency
rate $b_i$ without sacrificing the closed form.

The Monte Carlo evidence in @sec-simulations confirms $\sqrt{n}$ convergence
for all parameters and shows that the exponential frontier ranks units
almost as accurately as the correctly specified Gamma model when the true
shape lies near unity. Two limitations follow directly. First, the
rate–shape pair $(\alpha, b)$ is weakly identified along the
$\alpha \to 0$, $b \to \infty$ ridge: this manifests as persistent
under-coverage of delta-method intervals for $\hat\alpha$, and we recommend
profile-likelihood inference whenever inference on $\alpha$ is of
substantive interest. Second, the model treats the sample as a pooled
cross-section, with $u_i$ independent of $\mathbf{x}_i$; extending the
closed-form likelihood to genuine panel structures with a time-invariant
firm effect distinct from time-varying inefficiency is the most immediate
next step.

The empirical application reinforces both points. On the @hausman1984
patent panel the exponential frontier is precisely estimated and reproduces
the textbook R&D elasticity, while the homogeneous Gamma fit cannot
disentangle $\alpha$ from $b$ — an outcome anticipated by the curvature
analysis. The scaling specification (@sec-scaling) resolves the
difficulty by tying $b_i$ to firm characteristics, illustrating how the
closed form admits inefficiency determinants without simulation. Two
further extensions are natural: a zero-inflated frontier for settings with
structural non-participation, and Bayesian estimation —
following @mutzBornmannDaniel2017 in the count-data SFA setting — that
bypasses the flat-$\alpha$ direction by integrating over $\alpha$ rather
than profiling it out.



\appendix

# Proofs {#sec-app-proofs .appendix}

This appendix collects the proofs of the propositions stated in @sec-model
and the derivational arguments supporting the existence claims of
@sec-existence. The headline results (PMFs, efficiency-score formulas,
moment expressions) and their interpretive discussion remain in the main
text; only the technical arguments justifying them are lifted here.

## Proof of Proposition @prp-pmf (Marginal PMF) {#sec-app-pmf-proof}

*Production case.* Marginalising the conditional Poisson with $e^{-u_i}$
against the Gamma density,

$$
\Pr(Y_i = y \mid \mathbf{x}_i,\, \alpha,\, b)
= \frac{b^\alpha}{y!\,\Gamma(\alpha)}
  \int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda_i e^{-u}}\,
    \lambda_i^y\, e^{-yu}\, u^{\alpha-1}\, e^{-bu}\,\mathrm{d}u,
$$ {#eq-marginalization}

with $\lambda_i = \lambda(\mathbf{x}_i;\,\boldsymbol{\beta})$ entering through
$a_i = \log\lambda_i$. Expand $e^{-\lambda_i e^{-u}}$ as its Maclaurin series
in $\lambda_i e^{-u}$:

$$
e^{-\lambda_i e^{-u}}
\;=\; \sum_{j=0}^\infty \frac{(-1)^j \lambda_i^j e^{-ju}}{j!}
\;=\; \sum_{j=0}^\infty \frac{(-1)^j e^{j a_i} e^{-ju}}{j!}.
$$ {#eq-maclaurin}

The integral of the absolute value of the $j$-th term is
$e^{ja_i}\Gamma(\alpha)/[j!(y+b+j)^\alpha]$. Since $(y+b+j)^{-\alpha} \leq
b^{-\alpha}$ for all $y \geq 0$, $j \geq 0$ and $\alpha > 0$, summing
over $j$ gives
$\Gamma(\alpha)\sum_{j=0}^\infty e^{ja_i}/[j!\,(y+b+j)^\alpha]
\leq \Gamma(\alpha)\,b^{-\alpha}\sum_{j=0}^\infty e^{ja_i}/j!
= \Gamma(\alpha)\,b^{-\alpha}\,e^{e^{a_i}} < \infty$
for all finite $a_i$, so Fubini's theorem permits the exchange of sum and
integral. Substituting @eq-maclaurin into @eq-marginalization and integrating
term by term — using
$\int_0^\infty u^{\alpha-1}\, e^{-(y + b + j)u}\,\mathrm{d}u = \Gamma(\alpha)/(y+b+j)^\alpha$
and cancelling $\Gamma(\alpha)$ — gives @eq-pmf-gamma.

*Cost case.* The analogous marginalisation with $\lambda_i e^{+u}$ in place
of $\lambda_i e^{-u}$ yields

$$
\Pr(Y_i = y \mid \mathbf{x}_i,\, \alpha,\, b)
= \frac{b^\alpha}{y!\,\Gamma(\alpha)}
  \int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda_i e^{u}}\,
    \lambda_i^y\, e^{yu}\, u^{\alpha-1}\, e^{-bu}\,\mathrm{d}u,
$$ {#eq-gamma-integral-cost}

which collects to @eq-pmf-gamma-cost on writing
$\lambda_i^y\, e^{yu} = e^{a_i y}\, e^{yu}$ and
$e^{-\lambda_i e^{u}} = \exp(-e^{a_i + u})$. The integrand is integrable on
$(0, \infty)$: as $u \to \infty$ the double-exponential factor
$\exp(-e^{a_i + u})$ dominates the algebraic-times-exponential growth from
$u^{\alpha-1}\, e^{(y-b)u}$ for any finite $b$ and $y \geq 0$; near
$u = 0^+$ the prefactor $\exp(-e^{a_i + u})$ is continuous and bounded on
any neighbourhood $[0, u_*]$, so the integrand is bounded by a constant
multiple of $u^{\alpha-1}$, which is integrable for every $\alpha > 0$.
The Maclaurin expansion
$\exp(-e^{a_i + u}) = \sum_{j \geq 0} (-1)^j e^{j(a_i + u)}/j!$ that delivered
the production series cannot be integrated term by term here, because the
resulting inner integrals
$\int_0^\infty u^{\alpha-1}\, e^{(y + j - b)u}\,\mathrm{d}u$ diverge for every
$j \geq b - y$. No convergent term-by-term series via this Maclaurin
expansion is therefore available, and direct numerical quadrature of
@eq-pmf-gamma-cost is required.


## Proof of Proposition @prp-te (Conditional efficiency score) {#sec-app-te-proof}

In either orientation the conditional expectation is the ratio
$$
\mathrm{E}\!\left[e^{-u_i}\mid Y_i = y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i\right]
= \frac{\displaystyle\int_0^\infty e^{-u}\,
        f(Y_i = y_i \mid u,\, \mathbf{x}_i)\, f(u)\,\mathrm{d}u}
       {\displaystyle\int_0^\infty
        f(Y_i = y_i \mid u,\, \mathbf{x}_i)\, f(u)\,\mathrm{d}u},
$$
whose denominator is the marginal PMF from @prp-pmf and whose numerator
carries an additional factor of $e^{-u}$.

*Production case.* The extra $e^{-u}$ replaces $e^{-(y_i+b)u}$ by
$e^{-(y_i+b+1)u}$ in @eq-marginalization. The same Maclaurin expansion and
term-by-term integration as in the proof of @prp-pmf — with $(y_i + b + j)$
replaced by $(y_i + b + 1 + j)$, the dominating sum still bounded by
$\Gamma(\alpha)\,b^{-\alpha}\,e^{e^{a_i}}$ since $(y_i+b+1+j)^{-\alpha} \leq
b^{-\alpha}$, so Fubini again applies — gives
$$
\int_0^\infty e^{-u}\, f(Y_i = y_i \mid u,\, \mathbf{x}_i)\, f(u)\,\mathrm{d}u
\;=\; \frac{b^\alpha e^{a_i y_i}}{y_i!}
  \sum_{j=0}^\infty \frac{(-1)^j e^{j a_i}}{j!\,(y_i+b+1+j)^\alpha}.
$$
Dividing by @eq-pmf-gamma and cancelling $b^\alpha e^{a_i y_i}/y_i!$ yields
@eq-te-gamma.

*Cost case.* The extra $e^{-u}$ shifts the linear-in-$u$ exponent in
@eq-gamma-integral-cost from $(y_i - b)u$ to $(y_i - b - 1)u$, giving
$$
\int_0^\infty e^{-u}\, f(Y_i = y_i \mid u,\, \mathbf{x}_i)\, f(u)\,\mathrm{d}u
\;=\; \frac{b^\alpha e^{a_i y_i}}{y_i!\,\Gamma(\alpha)}
  \int_0^\infty u^{\alpha-1}\,
     \exp\!\bigl((y_i - b - 1)u - e^{a_i + u}\bigr)\,\mathrm{d}u.
$$
The double-exponential factor $\exp(-e^{a_i + u})$ damps the integrand for
any real coefficient on $u$, so the numerator is finite by the same argument
as @eq-pmf-gamma-cost. Dividing by @eq-pmf-gamma-cost and cancelling the
prefactor yields @eq-te-gamma-cost.


## Well-definedness of the PMF and log-likelihood for all $b > 0$ {#sec-app-likelihood}

The PMFs @eq-pmf-gamma and @eq-pmf-gamma-cost are well-defined for all
$b > 0$. In the cost integral @eq-gamma-integral-cost, the
double-exponential factor $\exp(-e^{a_i + u})$ dominates the
algebraic-times-exponential factor $u^{\alpha-1}\, e^{(y-b)u}$ as
$u \to \infty$ regardless of the sign of $y - b$; integrability at
$u = 0^+$ follows because the integrand is bounded by a constant multiple
of $u^{\alpha-1}$ in a neighbourhood of zero, which is integrable for any
$\alpha > 0$. For the production series @eq-pmf-gamma the ratio of
successive term magnitudes is $|t_{j+1}|/|t_j| = e^{a_i}\,[(y+b+j)/(y+b+j+1)]^\alpha
/(j+1) \to 0$ as $j \to \infty$, so the series converges absolutely with
no restriction on $b$.

Because the log-likelihood depends only on the PMF, it is well-defined for
all $b > 0$ in both orientations. The regularity conditions of
@neweyMcFadden1994 [Theorems 2.5 and 3.3] — identifiability, compactness
of a set containing the true parameter, continuity with a uniform
law-of-large-numbers bound, and non-singular Fisher information —
therefore deliver consistency and $\sqrt{n}$-asymptotic normality of the
MLE. In the Gamma frontier these conditions hold provided $\alpha$ is
bounded away from zero and $b$ is bounded away from zero and infinity; the
shape parameter $\alpha$ may be weakly identified in small samples or
shallow panels, a phenomenon documented in @sec-simulations.


## Existence of conditional moments $\mathrm{E}[e^{r u_i} \mid y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i]$ {#sec-app-condmoments}

The conditional efficiency scores @eq-te-gamma and @eq-te-gamma-cost are
*conditional* moments of $e^{-u_i}$ given $y_i$, not the unconditional
moments of the Gamma random variable. The two are not interchangeable:
conditioning on $y_i$ inserts a Poisson-likelihood factor into the
conditional density that strictly enlarges the set of $r$ for which
$\mathrm{E}[e^{r u_i}\mid y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i]$ is finite.

In the production frontier,
$Y_i \mid u_i \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda_i e^{-u_i})$ implies the posterior
density @eq-post-prod. As $u_i \to \infty$ the factor
$\exp(-\lambda_i e^{-u_i})$ tends to $1$, so the tail is Gamma-like with
rate $b + y_i$: more precisely, given $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists
$M < \infty$ such that
$1 - \varepsilon \leq \exp(-\lambda_i e^{-u_i}) \leq 1$ for all
$u_i \geq M$. Hence
$\int_M^\infty u^{\alpha-1}\, e^{(r-b-y_i)u}\, \exp(-\lambda_i e^{-u})\,
\mathrm{d}u$ is finite iff $\int_M^\infty u^{\alpha-1}\, e^{(r-b-y_i)u}\,
\mathrm{d}u$ is, which holds iff $r < b + y_i$. The conditional moment
$\mathrm{E}[e^{r u_i}\mid y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i]$ is therefore finite if
and only if $r < b + y_i$, which is automatic at $r = -1$ (the value
entering @eq-te-gamma) for any $b > 0$ and $y_i \geq 0$.

In the cost frontier,
$Y_i \mid u_i \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda_i e^{+u_i})$ delivers the
posterior density @eq-post-cost. The factor $\exp(-\lambda_i e^{u_i})$
decays *super-exponentially* as $u_i \to \infty$ — it dominates any
polynomial or exponential growth — and at $u_i = 0^+$ the integrand
behaves like $u_i^{\alpha-1}\, \exp(-\lambda_i)$, integrable for every
$\alpha > 0$. Both tails of the integrand defining
$\mathrm{E}[e^{r u_i}\mid y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i]$ are therefore finite for
*every* $r \in \mathbb{R}$, regardless of $b$. The cost-frontier
efficiency score is well-defined for all $b > 0$, in marked contrast to
the unconditional MGF $\mathrm{E}[e^{u_i}] = (b/(b-1))^\alpha$, which
requires $b > 1$.

In each orientation the denominator of the conditional expectation is the
marginal PMF from @prp-pmf, which is strictly positive for every
$y_i \in \mathbb{N}_0$ and every $(b, \alpha) \in (0,\infty)^2$; the
ratio defining the conditional moment is therefore unambiguous.

In short, the non-existence of the unconditional moment
$\mathrm{E}[e^{u_i}]$ when $b \leq 1$ does *not* propagate to the
conditional. Conditioning strictly enlarges the parameter region in which
moments are finite, because the Poisson likelihood supplies additional
damping in the right tail of $u_i$ that the Gamma density alone does not.
